CL 605.desc: Homogeneous reactor design and analysis-I: Ideal reactors, Review of isothermal design for batch, semi-batch and flow reactors, Multiple reactions and reaction networks: Yield-selectivity concepts, Wei-Prater analysis for first order networks, reaction networks of general order, Reactor energy balance and its applications to reactor design and analysis. Homogeneous reactor design and analysis-II: Non-ideal reactors- Review of the basic concepts of residence time distributions, single parameter models for real reactor behavior, Macromixing and micromixing, segregated flow model and Zweitering`s analysis of maximum mixedness, IEM and other models for micromixing. Heterogeneous reactors-I: Gas-solid systems- Review of kinetics of gas-solid catalytic reactions with and without diffusion limitations, Reactor design for fixed and fluidized bed reactors, Selected case studies, Non-catalytic gas-solid reactions: review of kinetics; reactor design case studies.Heterogeneous reactors-II: Gas-liquid systems- Basic theories of mass transfer with chemical reaction model systems and model reactors, Reactor design for mechanically agitated and bubble column reactors. Selected case studies.

CL 607.desc: Review of Basic Postulates, Maxwell`s relations, Legendre Transformation, Pure Component properties, Theory of corresponding states, real fluids Equilibrium, Phase Rule, Single component phase diagrams, Introduction to Multicomponent Multiphase equilibrium, introduction to Classical Mechanics, quantum Mechanics, Canonical Ensemble, Microcanonical Ensemble, Grand Canonical Ensemble, Boltzmann, Fermi-dirac and Bose Einstein Statistics,Fluctuations,Monoatomic and Diatomic Gases, introduction to Classical StatisticalMechanics, phase space, liouville equation, Crystals, Intermolecular forces and potential energy functions, imperfect Monoatomic Gases, Molecular theory of corresponding states, introduction to Molecular Simulations, Mixtures, partial molar properties, Gibbs Duhems equations, fugacity and activity coefficients, Ideal and Non-ideal solutions, Molecular theories of activity coefficients, lattice models, multiphase Multicomponent phase equilibrium, VLE/SLE/LLE/VLLE, Chemical Equilibrium and Combined phase and reactionequilibria.

CL 611.desc: 1. Introduction to electrochemistry and examples of electrochemical systems, Electrochemical cells and reactions.2. Non-faradic processes, Electrode-solution interface, and double layer capacitance.3. Factors affecting rates of electrode reactions ( Mass transfer controlled reactions and coupled chemical reactions)4. Thermodynamics of electrochemical systems, cell potentials, liquid junction potentials etc.5. Conductance, transference and mobility6. Kinetics of electrode reactions, Butler-Volmer model and Microscopic theories for charge transfer7. Mass transfer by migration and diffusion 9. Potential step Methods (chronoamperometry)- (Nernstian, Quasireversible and irreversible systems).10. Potential sweep techniques (reversible, irreversible and quasi reversible techniques) and cyclic voltammetry12. Electrochemical Impedance spectroscopy13. Bulk electrolysis14. Mechanisms of electrochemical reactions

CL 656.desc: Colloidal materials; Brownian motion and Brownian flocculation, Colloidal and interfacial forces, Interfaces, interfacial tension and contact angle, thermodynamics of interfaces, Stability of colloidal dispersions and interfaces, Electrokinetic phenomena and electroviscous flows, Capillary hydrostatics and thin films, Industrial applications.

CL 653.desc:
This course will deal with theory, tools and techniques for estimation of
process states (variables) in the presence of uncertainty. This
uncertainty is in the form of uncertainty (noise) in measurements as well
as disturbances/noise in the process. The theory covered will deal with
estimation for dynamic systems for both linear and nonlinear processes, as
well as for gaussian and non-gaussian noise characteristics. Issues
related to constraints on process states (constrained estimation), and
delayed and multirate measurements will also be dealt with. It is expected
that this course will expose the student to not only the well-accepted
basics of estimation, but also to recent advances in this area. Throughout
the course, the main ideas will be illustrated using matlab-based
examples. The following topics will be covered: (i) Linear Optimal
(Kalman) Filtering: Least squares formulation, Stochastic formulation,
Riccatti equation and solution techniques, Sensitivity and sub-optimal
filtering, Smoothing. (ii) Nonlinear Filtering with Gaussian Noise:
Extended Kalman filtering, Unscented Kalman filtering. (iii) Particle
Filtering: Sequential Monte-Carlo (SMC) methods, Markov processes, SMC
based particle filtering, Resampling-MOVE algorithm, Importance sampling,
SMC based smoothing, Self organizing state space models, Regularization in
particle filtering. (iv) Miscellaneous Topics: Deterministic linear and
nonlinear observers, Combined parameter and state estimation, Constrained
moving horizon estimators, Incorporation of delayed and multirate
measurements.

CL 669.desc:
The course will be an introduction to the methodology, skills, and "competencies" required for target/objective oriented R&D in creation of Chemical Technology embedded "Products". The "Products" under consideration could be consumer goods, foods, specialty chemicals, equipment or devices. The course will include case studies demonstrating the process of Product R&D, as well as application of rigorous Scientific and Chemical Engineering principles, to solve problems posed by real life products. Apart from class room lectures, the students will have to carry out short term assignments/projects. The course would be of benefit to
those interested in pursuing careers in R&D or Product Innovation. 

(A few interested 2nd, 3rd,4th Year B.Tech students will be permitted to opt for the course, although it is open, primarily to 5th year Dual Degree & PG students.)

CL 673.desc:
Single phase turbulence
Introduction to stability and transition. Vorticity dynamics. Origin and characteristics of turbulence. Spatial and temporal correlations. Time and length scales in turbulence. Momentum transport and concept of Reynolds stress. Kinetic Energy of turbulence. Spectral dynamics and energy cascading.

Multiphase dynamics
Properties of dispersed phase flows. Dilute versus dense phase flows. Interphase coupling between particles/droplets-fluid for mass, momentum, and energy transport. Particle-particle interactions. Phenomena of particle clustering, turbulence attenuation and augmentation, turbophoresis.

Modeling and Computational methods
Lagrangian-Eulerian and Eulerian-Eulerian approach to model multiphase turbulent flow. 
Introduction to direct numerical simulation using spectral method.
Hard sphere and discrete element model (DEM) to address dispersed phase interaction.

CL 685.desc: This course will cover the basics of engineering principles and biological concepts to develop the next generation of human-relevant platforms, known as Novel Alternative Methods (NAMs) for identifying and testing new drugs. Students will master the fundamentals of New Approach Methodologies(NAMs) and Microphysiological Systems (MPS).This includes an in-depth study of technologies such as organ-on-chipdevices, 3D-printed tissue constructs, and multicellular organoids,and howthese innovations are actively transforming the landscape of preclinicalresearch. A core focus is placed on the integration of diverse disciplines,including fluid dynamics, microenvironmental control, biomaterials science, and cell biology, to engineer miniaturized tissues. The goal is to successfully replicate the essential biochemical, mechanical, and functional characteristics of human organs in a controlled environment. The syllabus will also provide hands-on exposure to crucial biofabrication technologies. These include microfluidics, soft lithography, rapid prototyping, and 3D bioprinting. Students will learn how to leverage these techniques to design and build in vitro microphysiological systems.Upon successful completion of the course, students will possess the skillsto conceptualize, design, and execute human-relevant alternative modelsthat are directly applicable to modern drug discovery pipelines.The course will have the following modules:MODULE 1: Foundations & Context MODULE 2: Engineering Principles for NAMs MODULE 3: Fundamentals of Fabrication for NAMs MODULE 4: Biology of NAMs MODULE 5: Drug Screening, Toxicology & Validation, including in silico methods.A more detailed description can be found in the Annexure.

CL 683.desc:
Lectures of 1.5 h each
1. History of microfluidics and overview of its applications - Evolution of
Miniaturization, Device types, Research groups; Major applications in
the areas of Chemical, Materials, and Biomedical engineering (Lectures
1 and 2)
2. Designing of microfluidic devices - Geometries, Physics of fluid flows
in microfluidic channels, Design and simulation software (Lectures 3 and 4)
3. Device fabrication techniques - Materials for fabrication,
Photolithography, and Soft lithography (Lectures 5-7)
4. Droplet microfluidics - Introduction and Applications (Lecture 8)
5. Droplet generation techniques - Device geometries for generation, Flow
control, Droplet manipulation, Lab Demo (Lectures 9, 10)
6. Physics of droplet and jet formation - Dimensionless numbers,
Governing equations, Simulations strategies (Lectures 11, 12)
7. Droplet microfluidics for Material synthesis: Emulsions (single and
double), Particles (Multicompartment, Janus), Capsules, and FibersFabrication methods, Solidification Techniques, Material selection, Lab
Demo (Lectures 13-15)
8. Droplet microfluidics for Reaction engineering: Microreactors
Applications, Heat and Mass Transfer and Reaction kinetics in droplets,
Techniques for detection and analysis in droplets (Lectures 16, 17)
9. Droplet microfluidics for Drug delivery - Applications, Fabrication of
drug delivery vehicles viz hydrogels, nanoparticles, liposomes, Factors
controlling drug loading and release (Lectures 18-20)
10. Scaling up of microfluidic systems: Challenges and Emerging
Technologies (Lectures 21, 22)
Demo lectures: 2; Project presentation lectures: 2; Total: 26 lectures

CL 665.desc:
Part I: Overview and basics (~4 hours)
 Basics of sustainability and sustainable development
 Brundtland commission report, Millennium Ecosystems Assessment, IPCC report
 Evolution from green engineering to sustainable engineering
 Motivating examples from chemical as well as other branches of engineering
Part II: Sustainability quantification (~15 hours)
 Why quantification is important?
 Different quantification methods:
o Brief review of well established economic indicators
o Footprint analysis (carbon footprint, water footprint)
o Life cycle inventory and assessment
o Ecological/environmental carrying capacity
o Information theory based indicators for dynamic systems
o Resource use intensity
o Exergy, emergy etc.
o Social indicators
Part III: Design of sustainable systems (~15 hours)
 Importance of incorporating sustainability in design
 Examples of sustainable designs
 Design methods for sustainability
o Moving from quantification to decision making (design)
o LCA
o System dynamics models
o Other methods
 1-2 Hands-on activities using freely available tools such as the OpenLCA
(http://www.openlca.org/)
Part IV: Case studies: Sustainable design in chemical engineering and other allied fields (~ 6 hours)
 Examples of performing sustainable design from different fields such as a energy, water,
material use, solvent selection, ionic liquids
 Guest lectures by domain experts (e.g. faculty from CTARA, HSS or from outside of IITB), and
demonstration using educational modules (e.g. Center for Sustainable Engineering,
http://www.csengin.org/csengine/)

CL 688.desc: Intorduction-History and Relation of Artificial Intelligence (AI) to Process Engineering; Knowledge Representation I- Predicate calculus and Semantic Networks; Search-forward/Backward. depth/breadth/best - first search; Production Systems; History; Components; Knowledge Representation II - Frames, Objects; Inexact Reasoning - Introduction, Blackboard Architecture; Expert Systems - Applications to Industry' Programming Languages; Expert System Shells; Neural Nets - Introduction and Application to Process Engineering.

CL 706.desc:
Molecular Structure of Matter  states of matter, solids and bonding, amorphous solids, density and distribution functions of liquids, non-ideal gases, kinetic theory of gases, Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution, molecular origins of transport.
Solution Thermodynamics  review of the laws of thermodynamics and the Boltzmann equation for entropy, chemical potential and its relation to equilibrium, ideal and non-ideal solutions, colligative properties, Debye Huckel Theory.
Theories of Chemical Reactions/Chemical Kinetics  mechanisms and rate laws, transition state theory, collision theory, activated complex theory, transport properties, catalysis, reactions in fluids, reactions on solid surfaces.

CL 702.desc:
Course Content: The students are required to attend lectures on a wide
variety of topics relevant to Chemical Engineering research and industry,
presented by persons of high standing in industry and other academic
institutions, faculty from the chemical Engineering and the other
Departments /Centres /Schools at IIT-B and PhD Students at advanced stages
in their Research. Assessment of the performance would be based on
attendance alone.

CL 701.desc: Errors and error control; Interpolation and optimization; Linear algebraic equations; Matrix inversion and matrix eigenvalues estimation; Solution of non-linear algebraic equations; Newton Raphson technique and homotopy; Numerical integration; Ordinary differential equations; Runge Kutta Techniques; Predictor corrector methods; solution of partial differential equations; implicit and explicit techniques; finite volume and finite element methods; Differential algebraic equations; Introduction to equation solving software like MATLAB, DSSL, LSODE etc.

CL 713.desc: Introduction to network and Motivation.Network as a model and a graph. Elements of basic graph theory.Simulation and analyses. Networks metrics  and operation. Analyses iof large netwroks.Sub-networks approach.Network decomposition -Motif finding Modularity.Large Network approach: Boolean dynamics modeling.Case studies- 1. Process networks; Sensor network, Reaction Networks, Water distribution networks.2, biological networks: Cellular networks, Disease Networks.Tools Matlab.

CL 716.desc:
Temporal patterns in chemical and biological systems  Introduction to temporally oscillating chemical and biological systems. Examples from chemistry such as CIDMA, Brusselator, B-Z reactions. Examples from biology such as circadian rhythms.  Experimental observations and measurements. Modeling and some analytical solutions to ODEs.
Spatio-temporal pattern formation and analytical solutions  Introduction to spatial pattern formation in chemical and biological systems. Reaction-diffusion systems. BZ- reactions, Turing patterns. Spatial patterns in reaction  diffusion-advection systems. Stationary and travelling waves. Modeling of spatio-temporal patterns. Some analytical solutions to PDEs. 
Computer simulation of spatio-temporal dynamics  Numerical solutions to PDEs. Simulating the evolution of spatio-temporal patterns in chemical and biological systems.

CM 507.desc:
Key topics:
     Crash 	course on Vector calculus
     Numerical solution of algebraic and transcendental equations, iterative algorithms, convergence. Optimisation and root finding algorithms for linear and nonlinear systems of equations.
     Numerical differentiation and integration: methods of integration and differentiation, order and accuracy, error estimation.
     Ordinary differential equations: order, linearity. Numerical solution: discretisation, accuracy and stability. Initial and boundary value 	problems; eigenvalue problems. Stiff equations.
     Partial differential equations. properties of partial differential 	equations: order, linearity / nonlinearity, well-posedness, 	ellipticity / hyperbolicity. Initial and boundary value problems.
     Numerical 	methods for partial differential equations: discretisation in space: 	(pseudo-)spectral, finite-difference / finite-volume; collocated 	and staggered grids; discretisation in time: implicit and explicit time-stepping. Error analysis of numerical methods: stability, dispersion, dissipation.
     Choosing numerical methods based on the properties of the governing 	equations: central and one-sided discretisations; high- and low- 	order discretisations; ENO/WENO schemes.
     Fluid dynamics. Lagrangian and Eulerian descriptions of fluid dynamics, Reynolds transport theorem, constitutive relations, Reynolds transport theorem, conservation of mass, momentum, energy;  nondimensional parameters.
Advanced topics:
     Approximations of the fully compressible governing equations: anelastic, Boussinesq / incompressible, shallow-water. The scales of fluid flow (e.g. in the atmosphere: micro-, meso-, synoptic, planetary scale).
     Hierarchy of numerical methods: Direct numerical simulation (DNS), large-eddy simulation (LES), Reynolds averaged Navier-Stokes simulation (RANS); domains of application.

CM 617.desc: * Introduction to Sustainability and Sustainable development       * The Science and economics of Sustainability, Key concepts and drivers, Drivers for Unsustainability - Externality, Unsustainable Business models, Tragedy of Commons       * Overview and origin of sustainability: Brundtland Commission report, Millennium Ecosystem Assessment, Sustainable Development goals       * Measurement of Sustainability - Sustainability metrics, Tools, Indicators       * Sustainable design methodologies: Circular economy, industrial ecology, green engineering       * Economics and Policies and Regulations for sustainable development       * Sustainability in Practice - Sustainable Production and consumption, Sustainability in Business, Sustainability in Design       * Climate change and sustainability - Ethics and Equity of Climate change, Climate Financing , Climate change mitigation and adaptation from sustainability perspectives.

CL 899.desc: Context of communication: Recognizing our capability androles as professionalsScientific Method: Question and answer aspects oftechnical communication; Scientific Methodology and itsrelationship to technical communication;Surveying literature: Categories; reading and organizingscientific literature; search engines and tools.Listening and Note taking: 5-R method and mind-mapping.Technical writing: Report organization; Journal selection;Introduction, conclusion, and abstract writing.Speaking & Presentation skills: Organization ofpresentation slides (number, content, and formatting); Oralpresentations; Audience/context dependent practices; Nonverbal aspects: body language, eye-contact, personalappearance, facing large audience.Elevator pitch: Pitches for technical audience and policymakersWorkplace communication: Sensitivity towards genderand diversity; Email communication and netiquettes.Ethics in academic communication: Intellectual Property,copyrights and plagiarism; Authorship; Data ethics; Biasesand balanced criticism of literature;Suggested additional topics relevant to disciplines: Datarepresentation, Group discussion and interviews; accessiblescientific writing, report writing using LaTeX, Proofreading, etc

CM 704.desc:
Unit 1: A basic forward-backward optimization loop : In this unit we will setup a simple neural network that learns logic gates using logistic regression. The purpose of this simple exercise will be to familiarize ourselves with pytorch, how to implement the training and validation loops, and get an intuition for how the network learns.     

Unit 2: Identifying climate patterns from data : We will employ principal component analysis (PCA) to identify prominent modes of variability in the ocean-atmosphere system. We will isolate and identify modes of variability like El Nino and Indian Ocean Dipole from raw ocean data.     

Unit 3: Using deep neural networks for prediction tasks In this unit we will use an artificial neural network to learn relationships between hazards and their drivers. We will see that different drivers might be important in different regions.     

Unit 4: Gap filling of data : Satellite-derived datasets inevitably have gaps in them. Convolutional neural networks (CNNs) have been shown to perform well on many vision tasks. We will use CNNs to fill such gaps in satellite-derived datasets.      

Unit 5: Emulating numerical models with neural operators : In this unit we will extend CNNs to state-of-the-art architectures like neural operators. We will emulate the three dimensional oceanic or atmospheric circulation using one such neural operator.

CM 619.desc:
Review of basic concepts: Pressure, Temperature, Density, State variables and state
functions, Composition of Atmosphere, Ideal gas laws, Equation of State of Air, Mixture
of ideal gases, Composition of Ocean, Equation of State of Seawater, Compressibility &
Thermal expansion coefficient of Seawater, Hydrostatic equilibrium 
First and Second Law of Thermodynamics: Work, Internal energy, Heat, First law,
Enthalpy, Water vapor in air, The Carnot cycle, Entropy, Second law, Clausius-Clapeyron
relationship, Thermodynamics of the Climate system, Global energy and entropy balances
(all in context of climate sciences)
Thermodynamic processes in the atmosphere: Dry and moist processes, Isobaric cooling,
Dew and Frost points, Horizontal mixing, Vertical mixing, Adiabatic expansion,
Adiabatic ascent and air saturation, Pseudo-adiabatic process
Thermodynamic processes in the ocean: Adiabatic processes in Ocean, Potential
temperature considering Salinity, Potential density, Thermohaline circulation
Stability in the atmosphere: Stability Criteria, Lapse Rates, Oscillations in a stable layer,
Conditional stability, Convective Available Potential Energy, Processes inducing changes
in stability
Stability in the Ocean: Stability Criteria, Inclusion of Salinity gradient, Lapse rate,
Oscillations in stable layer, Processes producing changes in stability
Cloud microphysics: Nucleation of Water vapor condensation, Warm clouds and growth
of Cloud droplets in warm clouds, Growth by Condensation, Collection, CollisionCoalescence, Cold clouds and growth of ice particles, Lightning and thunderstorm,
Artificial modification of clouds and precipitation
Thermodynamics & climate change: Climate energetics, Radiative forcing, Climate
feedbacks, Plancks feedback, Water vapor feedback, Cloud feedback, Albedo feedback,
Climate sensitivity

CS 101.desc: This course provides an introduction to problem solving with computers using a modern language such as Java or C/C++. Topics covered will include :* Utilization : Developer fundamentals such as editor, integrated programming environment, Unix shell, modules, libraries.* Programming features : Machine representation, primitive types,arrays and records, objects, expressions, control statements, iteration,procedures, functions, and basic i/o.* Applications : Sample problems in engineering, science, text processing, and numerical methods.

CS 215.desc: The role of statisitcs. Graphical and numerical methods for describing and summarising data. Probability. Population distributions. Sampling variability and sampling distributions. Estimation using a single sample. Hypothesis testing a single sample. Comparing two populations or treatments. Simple linear regression and correlation. Case studies.

CS 105.desc:
Propositions and predicates, proofs and proof techniques. Sets, relations and functions, cardinality, basic counting.
Posets and lattices: Dilworth`s theorem, inversion and distributive lattices.
Graph theory : paths, cycles, trees, connectivity.
Group theory : Lagrange`s theorem, homomorphisms, applications.

regular expressions. Properties of regular languages.

solvability, Turing enumerability, acceptability and

Perfectly secret encryption: one time pad, limitations of perfect security

Vulnerabilities, risks, attacks, defences, forensics. 
Examples and applications of Public Key Cryptography and Secret Key Cryptography. Crypto hash, digital signatures, digital certificates and PKI. 
Basic Authentication and Key Agreement protocols, SSL/TLS and Kerberos. Vulnerabilities and attacks on TCP, DNS, ARP, etc. and DDoS attacks.

CS 616.desc:
Parallelizing Compilers - motivation and overview, structure of a parallelizing compiler; review of code optimization techniques in compilers for sequential machines. 

Parallelism detection - data dependence, direction vectors, loop carried and loop independent dependences; tests for data dependence and their applicability, construction of data dependence graph. 

Control dependence and control dependence graph. 

Restructuring transformations and automatic extraction of parallelism; representation of iteration spaces of multiply nested loops; loop based transformations such as loop distribution, loop coalescing, loop inter-change and cycle shrinking transformation. 

Introduction to machine models and associated algorithms for parallel code generation and task graph construction. 

Interprocedural analysis - aliasing information, summary data flow analysis, interprocedural constant propagation, interprocedural data dependence analysis and parallelisation of call statements. 

Partitioning and Scheduling of a task graph; models for communication, partitioning and scheduling algorithms, dynamic scheduling algorithms, target code generation. 

Case studies of contemporary parallelising compiler efforts.

CS 602.desc: Selected algorithms from areas such as graph and combinatorial algorithms, computational geometry, string processing algorithms, algebraic and number theoretic algorithms, linear programming and combinatorial optimization.

CS 604.desc:
Min-max results. Dilworth"s theorem, Hall"s theorem. 

Menger"s theorem. 

Topics on tournaments. Perfect graphs. The weak perfect graph theorem. 

Ramsey theory. 

Set Systems. Sperner, Erdos-Rado and the Erdos-Ko-Rado theorems. 

Colex orders and the Kruskal-Katona theorem. 

The probabilistic method. Some sample applications of the probabilistic method. Second moment method and applications. 

Matroid theory. The theory of designs. 

Combinatorics and Algorithm design. The Lovasz-theta function and its use in designing algorithms.

CS 618.desc:
Prerequisites. First course in compiler construction. 
 
Mathematical Foundations of Program Analysis Lattices: partial orders and approximations, bounded lattices, complete lattices. Function spaces monotonicity, distributivity, separability, boundedness. Fixed Point Computations: least fixed point and induction, greatest fixed point and co-induction. 
Abstract interpretation: concretisation and abstraction functions, Galois connection. Alias analysis of heap data Type of alias information: Context sentitive/insensitive, flow sensitive.insensitive, may/must aliases. 
Representations of heap properties: shape graphs, alias graphs, storage shape graphs, k-limiting, compact representations, access graphs. 
Data flow analysis for aliases. 

Liveness analysis of heap data Shape graphs and heap safety automaton based approach. Static type inferencing of flow sensitive polymorphic types. Type inferencing for validation and type inferencing for optimization. Data flow analysis for monomorphic languages. Extensions for polymorphic types, type graphs and operations. 
Interprocedural data flow analysis Context sensitive/insensitive analysis. Functional approach. Call strings approach. Call strings graphs. Call strings approach for k-bounded problems.

Physical storage and indexing structures, Query processing algorithms, Query optimization, Transaction processing and serializability, Concurrency Control, Recovery.

Emerging database trends.

CS 632.desc: The course will be based primarily on research papers covering the following topics:        Top-k query processing including skylines, Fagin"s algorithm and quality of top-k. Materialized views and its implications in ranking. Advanced query optimization, including database tuning. Adaptive query processing and optimization; Transaction and query processing on main-memory and columnar databases; Data streams and stream management systems; Handling uncertain data; Applications of declarative querying/optimization outside of database applications. While this area has seen many traditional methods, recently ML based techniques have shown empirical superiority to the traditional statistical methods.

CS 769.desc:
Intention is to cover a reasonable subset of the following topics. 

Continuous and Discrete Optimization are two important pillars of machine learning. Continuous optimization typically occurs in learning model parameters while discrete optimization problems appear in inference and auxiliary tasks such as feature selection, data subset selection, model compression etc.

In the first part of this course, we will cover continuous optimization with applications in machine learning. Topics to discuss will include Convexity, Gradient Methods, Proximal algorithms, Stochastic and Online Variants of mentioned methods, Coordinate Descent Methods, Subgradient Methods, Non-Convex Optimization, FrankWolfe, Accelerated Methods, Lagrange
and Fenchel Duality, Second-Order Methods, Quasi-Newton Methods, Gradient-Free and Zero-Order Optimization. We will also cover some advanced topics including non-convex optimization, alternating minimization algorithms and parallel/distributed variants of algorithms. We will ground all these algorithms with applications and loss functions in machine
learning (starting from logistic regression, linear regression, svms, matrix factorization right up to deep neural networks). Summarily,in Continuous Optimization we will cover topics such as Basics of Continuous Optimization,  Convexity,  Gradient Descent,  Projected/Proximal GD,  Subgradient Descent,  Accelerated Gradient Descent,   Newton & Quasi Newton,    Duality: Legrange, Fenchel, Coordinate Descent, Frank Wolfe,     Continuous Optimization in Practice, etc. 

The second part of this course will cover the fundamentals of discrete optimization. We will start with basic forms of combinatorial optimization (knapsack, s-t
cuts/paths, matchings and matroids) and then discuss submodular functions and their applications. We will cover algorithms for submodular minimization and maximization under various combinatorial constraints. We will also discuss discrete probabilistic models such as Determinantal Point Processes (DPPs). We will ground all these algorithmic and theoretical
results with real world applications in feature selection, summarization and diversified search, structured prediction, data subset selection and model compression. Summarily, in Discrete Optimization we will cover topics such as Linear Cost Problems with Applications in ML,  Matroids, Spanning Trees,  s-t paths, s-t cuts,   Matchings,   Covers (Set Covers, Vertex Covers, Edge Covers),  Non-Linear Discrete Optimization,   Submodular Functions and Applications in Machine Learning,   Submodularity and Convexity,   Submodular Minimization,   Submodular Maximization, Beyond Minimization and Maximization: Other forms of Submodular Optimization,   Discrete Optimization in Practice

CS 744.desc:
CPU scheduling, process management and inter-process
communication, memory management, thread-safe

CS 787.desc: The course deals with the extensive set of language engineering and design features that C++ Programming Language offers to develop complex programs, and their applications to systems software and tools.       (i) Components and modularity: Namespaces, Koneig lookup, scoping rules, initializers, different kinds of constructors/destructors, separate compilation, libraries, errors and exceptions.      (ii) Functions and control: Overloading and operator cascading, Lambda functions, parameter passing models, iterators, assignment semantics, dynamic dispatch/virtual table implementations.     (iii) Concurrency and distributed programming: Threading, Synchronization, Parallel and distributed programs     (iv) Generic Programming: Building templates, and the feature set in the standard template library, variadic functions and macros, variadic templates, template metaprogramming     (v) Data, streams and conversions: Types and typing rules, type inferencing, type-safety issues, Memory models,  strings, regular expressions, containers, ranges, file streams, string streams.     (vi) Patterns and compositions: Design pattern implementations for patterns such as Strategy, Prototype, Builder, Visitor, Composite, Adapter, Proxy, Observer and Iterator.     (vii) Advanced Applications in C++: Engineering and implementations of programs utilizing the rich set of features and design patterns, applying them to systems software such as distributed publisher subscriber event services, sequential algorithmics, model-based tools such as model translators, analyzers, file systems, interpreters and execution engines of experimental languages and extended language features for specialized purposes.

CS6010.desc: Theory Content:     * Introduction: Motivation, brief history, various service (Software as a Service, Platform as a Service, Infrastructure as a Service etc) and deployment models (Public, Private, Hybrid, Community)     * Cloud Building Blocks and their Cloud manifestation     - Data Centers: Internals and their cloud manifestation in the form of regions, availability zones, SLAs, fault domains, placement groups, data residency etc     - Compute Virtualization: Bare metal vs hypervisors, Virtual machines, Containers. Cloud manifestations in the form of VM services, container runtimes, managed compute     - Internet & Networking: Internet & IP networking, DNS, NAT, public vs private networks. Cloud manifestations in the form of VPC, subnets, routing tables, internet gateways, NAT gateways, load balancers, CDNs     - Identity & Access Management (IAM): Users vs roles, permissions and policies, least privilege principle, API keys vs role-based access. Cloud manifestations in the form of IAM users, roles, policies, service identities     - Storage Technologies: Block storage, File storage, Object storage. Cloud manifestations in the form of block volumes, network file systems, object storage (S3 buckets), archival tiers     - Distributed Systems: Scalability, fault tolerance, consistency challenges. Cloud manifestations in the form of message queues, managed databases, stateless vs stateful services     * Cloud Operations     - Cost awareness: pay-as-you-go model, common cost pitfalls: Idle compute resources, data transfer (egress costs), storage tier misuse     - Security basics: Shared responsibility, security groups / firewall rules, data exposure risks (e.g., public buckets)     - Observability: Logs vs metrics, basic debugging workflow using logs and metrics     - API-Driven Cloud: Console vs Command Line Interface vs Software Development Kits, Infrastructure as API calls     * Emerging Trends: Hybrid multi-cloud, Serverless, Microservices, Cloud Native, Edge computing     Above theory content will be interspersed with hands-on components, introduced as demos or lab-assignments. These sessions will be conducted during class hours, after every 3-4 theory classes.     Hands-on Component: (based on Amazon AWS free-tier offering)     * Launching VMs and Containers in cloud     * Introduction to dockers     * Identity and Access Management (IAM) exploration via programmatic access to services     * Creating Virtual Private Cloud (VPC) and launching a distributed web application     * Extending applications to use load balancers, CDNs     * Attaching different types of storage to applications     * Extending applications to use message queues

CS6007.desc: Recent applications of ML (recommendation systems, NLP, RL etc) requiresolving large scale problems. One standard way to beat the curse-of-scale is tointroduce multiple agents and leverage collaboration. Hence, Multi-agent MLhas now become an important topic in AI. Right now, there is no course onMulti-agent ML at IITB. This course will introduce a variety of problems(some of them open to date) to the students and teach them tools andtechniques (algorithmic solutions) to address them.Module I: Federated Learning (FL)Basic Formulation, FedAvg algorithm, Non-convexity. Challenges in FL : (i)Heterogeneity (ii) Communication Bottleneck (iv) Adversarial Attacks (v)Data Privacy. To address these, we study (i) Clustering in FL, BoundedHeterogeneity control (ii) Local Averaging and data compression (deltaapproximate compressors) (iii) Robust estimators (Geometric Median,Trimmed Mean) and (iv) Differential Privacy. We study a variety of algorithmswith sharp theoretical guarantees.Module II: Multi Agent BanditsCollaborative Multi Agent Multi Arm Bandits. Gossip Type Algorithms,Modified Upper Confidence Bound, Regret Guarantees. FederatedBandits-Regret and Best Arm Identification. Heterogeneous Federated Bandits,Clustering and Personalization. Federated RL (TD and Q learning).Module III: Matching MarketsFormulation of matching markets with multiple agents and arms via a bipartitegraph, Stable matching, Matching Algorithms: Gale-Shapley, PreferenceLearning via a multi armed bandit framework, Connection to economicmarkets.Algorithms: (i) Collision avoidance Upper Confidence Bound (CA-UCB) (ii)Explore-Then-Gale Shapley (ETGS) (iii) Dominated Upper Confidence Bound(UCBD3).Variations: Dynamic Market, Structured Market, Robust Market

CS6008.desc:
Module1: Network Background (A quick overview)
Internet Protocol Stack, Link Layer technologies, Network Layer Protocols, Transport Layer Protocols, Popular Applications: Web, Email, Multimedia and SNMP

Hand-in-hand with theory, practical sessions involving usage of  packet capture/manipulation tools like Wireshark, Scapy and popular network commands like arp, ip, ping, ss, nc, dig, wget, curl, nmap etc will be covered.

Module2: Network Attacks and Defenses
Attacks at various layers of the protocol stack will be covered.
Link Layer: Eavesdropping via MAC flooding, ARP spoofing, VLAN Hopping, Wireless network Attacks
NetworkLayer: IP4/IPv6, ICMP, DHCP, Routing based Attacks including DOS
Transport Layer: UDP and TCP based Attacks including DOS
Application Layer: DNS, Email, SNMP, Multimedia based Attacks, including DOS

Practical sessions will explore a subset of these attacks hands-on

Module3:  Secure Network Protocols
Link layer: 802.1X, WEP/WPA, MAC-sec
Network layer: IPsec, IKE, openVPN
Transport layer: TLS
Application Layer: SSH, DNSsec , DNS cookies, Email Security (S/MIME, DKIM, DMARC, STARTTLS, SMTP-AUTH, IMPAS), SNMP security

Practical sessions will explore a subset of these protocols, configuration and exploration of the corresponding traffic via wireshark

Module4: Network Hardening
Defense in depth, Vulnerability Scanners, Secure network topologies (DMZ, Network Segmentation), Secure Network Access Control, Firewalls, IDS, Honeypots and Honeynets

Practical sessions will explore network security tools like openvas (network vulnerability scanner); iptables (firewall configuration), host and network intrusion detection (OSSEC and Snort)

DE 107.desc:
To look at retelling as a powerful means of examining, interpreting and understanding the present by exploring the resources of the past.In this module we will look at some of the reasons why we retell stories:To make old texts new. To interrogate inherited mythologies/perceptions/worldviews. To enjoy the gaps in the old stories and fill them in new ways.To make room for a multiplicity of narratives.To give voice to marginal/invisible/unjustly-treated characters.To raid the shared collective cultures we can all access.
To celebrate that great gift: freedom of interpretationTo understand who we are and where we come from.
We will look at some of the areas that are rich with possibility for creative retellings: mythologies, fairy tales, folktales. Each student will be encouraged to pick a favourite story and retell it over the duration of the course. This may well provide a base for further creative projects, collaborative, or individual.

DE 341.desc: Our access to our city is mediated by technologies, systems, conventions and most of all, by our embodied limits. This course proposes to introduce the city as our prime repository of design problems. Its purpose is to introduce students to ways in which they can begin to understand urban problems. The city offers problems which are at once of a social and technical nature, and introducing students to this area of interdisciplinary scholarship will be helpful to them in developing their own future work.This course introduces students to the city through lectures, films and assignments. Students will learn to inquire into the city around them and the many systems which constitute it as a social and physical environment. Assignments and projects will involve field work and class work to describe, diagnose and propose interventions at a human scale.At the end of this course, students will be have learnt (a) to read the city in terms of the many systems which co-exist within it, (b) techniques of mapping urban phenomena, and (c) several theoretical and conceptual perspectives from which the city has been examined in the interdisciplinary literature

The history, development and practitioners of various printing making techniques including hand printing and printing by press. Exploration of print making on different materials and using different kinds of inks. Understanding the difference between print-making and printing. Understanding print making as a tool of communication and design. The elective expects the development of a progressive portfolio  showing the developing explorations of the topic by each student.DeliverablesA portfolio of prints showcasing different techniques.A written document explaining the journey of each student and what is learned.Equipment required in the LabLino printer, rollers of various lengths and hardnesses, paper, linoleum sheets

DE 349.desc:
Background
Virtual Reality (VR) is reshaping the creative landscape, providing a canvas
that goes beyond traditional limitations. Artists are no longer confined to flat
surfaces or physical materials-they can paint in the air, sculpt in 360, and
animate immersive environments. By blending traditional artistic principles
with cutting-edge digital tools, VR offers a revolutionary immersive and
interactive expression platform.
As digital tools increasingly influence creative disciplines, this course aims to
equip students with the essential skills to adapt and innovate in this evolving
artistic landscape. VR technology breaks down artistic barriers, enabling fully
immersive experiences that open up new avenues for creativity, self-expression,
and personal growth.
Course Overview: Immersive Art in VR
The "Immersive Art in VR" course is designed for students eager to explore art
in a new dimension. It introduces participants to industry-leading VR creative
tools, allowing them to experiment with painting, sculpting, animation, and
calligraphy in a fully immersive environment. Students will learn how to
harness the boundless potential of VR as an artistic medium, pushing the limits
of imagination, interaction, and expression.
Throughout the course, students will:
Learn the fundamentals of VR art creation using industry-leading
tools.
Explore diverse artistic styles in VR, from dynamic 3D
painting to virtual sculpting and animation.
Create personal projects in VR, AR, or XR, choosing an art form of
interest.
Collaborate on group VR art installations to push creative and
technical boundaries.
This program delivers a transformative learning experience by leveraging VR`s
unique capabilities, fostering creativity, innovation, and emotional expression.
Additionally, the course incorporates creative art therapy principles,
acknowledging the power of art as a means for enhancing emotional well-being and cognitive development.
Learning Outcomes
Upon completion of this course, students will:
Master VR-based artistic tools to create digital paintings, 3D
sculptures, and interactive animations.
spaces.
Expand creative problem-solving skills by working in 360 virtual
Develop a portfolio of VR artworks, demonstrating expertise
lin immersive media.
Improve spatial reasoning and artistic visualization, critical for design,
gaming, and AR/VR industries.
Enhance emotional well-being through expressive creativity, drawing
from art therapy principles.
Engage in collaborative VR projects, strengthening communication
and teamwork skills.
Conclusion
This course is more than just a new way to create art-it`s a gateway into
the future of immersive creativity. By combining VR technology
with expressivę artistic techniques, students will gain practical skills in digital
artistry while benefiting from the therapeutic and cognitive advantages of
immersive experiences.
Whether students aim to explore art professionally, enter the XR industry, or
simply unlock new creative possibilities, this course provides the tools and
experience to push creative boundaries in the digital age.

DE 649.desc:
Covers the entire process from script to screen in process for an animation film (short or feature)
 Overview of the Process
 Preproduction, Production, Post Production
 A comparison between live action filmmaking& animation The primary difference being that an animation film tends to be pegged down pretty accurately by the end of the preproduction process itself whereas for a live action film it is close to completion only at the end of editing which is a post production process.
Preproduction

 Cleanups

DE 720.desc:
* Some aspects that will be covered in the course include: It begins with the fundamentals of colour theory and perception, helping students understand how colour influences visual impact, emotional response, and user experience. The module explores colour psychology, cultural meanings, and global market preferences, enabling students to design with sensitivity to diverse audiences.

      * Students will study how colour supports brand identity and market positioning, with case studies from iconic automotive brands. The course also integrates the study of Colour, Material, and Finish (CMF), focusing on how colour interacts with texture, light, and form. Trends and forecasting are introduced through resources like BASF and Axalta.

      * Through workshops and critiques, students will apply their knowledge by creating brand-specific colour palettes and CMF strategies for concept vehicles. The module emphasises practical skills and strategic thinking to prepare students for industry-relevant CMF design roles.

DE 714.desc:
Objective:
This course, through the integration of findings from critical readings, ethnographic research, mediated storytelling and hybrid prototyping, will speculate, imagine and prototype plausible futures. The course seeks to produce a diversity of what will? and what if? cultural provocations that deeply examine possible, desirable and undesirable futures. By the end of the course, students will be able to:  
     Gain knowledge of prior and related future-focused work and speculative/critical/fiction design.
     Gain the ability to read and discuss research papers from the design fiction literature.
     Gain an understanding of the key techniques of inversion, defamiliarization, uncertainty scenarios, and everyday practice to create objects, systems and experiences that create a discursive space for conversation, debate and understanding.
     Produce diegetic prototypes using various media and storytelling techniques.
     Articulate and critique the social and cultural, ethical and legal, health and environmental, political, and economic implications of emerging technologies.
     Develop evaluation/critique tools and frameworks.

Content:
     Introduction to Design Fiction, Critical Design and Speculative Design, citing examples from architecture, film, art, and literature.
     Introduction to futurisms in theory and fiction
     Survey of new media and emerging technologies
     Survey of narrative and production techniques used for creating Design Fiction, drawing on historical and recent works to see the methods in action.
     Socio-Technical Systems
     Narrative structures and storytelling techniques
     Diegetic prototyping

Methodology:
Classes will involve lectures, discussions, studio sessions, project presentations, critique sessions and occasional guest presentations. Students will participate in and lead in-class discussions and presentations. The students will be required to create Design Fiction artefact(s) to be presented at the end of the course.

Evaluation:
     Individual reading and discussion assignment
     Individual project

1. Early cinema: Evolution of film language and development of cinematic form
     2. Film theories: Introduction to major film theories and critical frameworks
     3. Storytelling in films: Narrative structures and techniques in cinematic storytelling
     4. Cinematic image making: Types of shots, framing, composition, and their usage in film language
     5. Editing: Continuity editing, montage theory, and constructive editing approaches
     6. Scriptwriting: Writing for cinema, screenplay structure, and storyboarding fundamentals
     7. Film production: Production processes, roles, workflow, and production management in filmmaking

DE 727.desc: The course discusses semantic and structural aspects of visual input, through series of project assignments. Using human information processing capabilities, the course looks back towards formal concepts such as visual complexity, visual interest and recall of forms. The first part covers topics such as spatio  temporal relationships in visual elements and its role in visual structure, forms of visual relationships, and its role in controlling visual complexity, interest and identity. Assignments are also based on application of Information theory to visual information. The second part discusses the role of characteristic features in object/form recognition and natural categorization process

DE 733.desc: Objective :* To understand the working of automobiles from a system-level approach through hands-on/field experience.    * To observe common technical issues in the selected category of vehicle.    * To get familiar with the engineering and technology side of automobiles and develop a broader system level understanding of mobility and vehicle technology    * To learn about the developments in various aspects of technology that led to the evolution of mobility and vehicle design       ** Course Contents :Some aspects that will be covered in the course include:    * Exposures to systems in a vehicle-power system: Gasoline, diesel, bio-diesel, electrical, hybrids, etc    * Transmission system clutch, gear trains, differential Suspension, steering, breaks, etc.    * Controls: Clutch, gear, accelerator    * Dashboard displays. HVAC   * Vehicle structure On Frames, monocoque.   * Modern assembly (robotic) and finishing technologies   * Packaging & Integrating Methodology, aerodynamics, ergonomics, Seating Systems, Trim Systems, Trim Systems, Safety & Homologation    * Reading and seminars.    * Visit to auto plants and use of interactive software.      ** Methodology and Deliverables :     * Develop a morphological system map of mobility vehicles by using gigamapping techniques.    * Select one of the following mobility systems: Bicycles, Motorcycles, Three-wheelers, Four-wheelers, and Commercial vehicles, and work for a week with a workshop specializing in one category.    * Complete literature review of the evolution of the previously selected sub-system. Make a poster (or presentation) highlighting key observations during the workshop and the literature review.    * Use modular playing devices to simulate the working principle of an automotive sub-system.

DEP410.desc:
The project could be any of the following:
a. Design project of student interest and/ or faculty interest and/ or industry project

b. Re-design project that relooks at an existing problem or situation c. Research project, delving into methodological or pedagogic issues d. Exploration project, exploring application possibilities in a new

technology or medium or variations

. This project would involve all aspects of the design process starting from problem identification to complete solution. The student is expected to make use of the knowledge and skills gained during the previous years to independently handle a design project.

. The project can be multi-disciplinary in nature inovolving many of these diciplines - Product Design, Interaction Design, Communication Design, Animation, etc.

. This project can also be sponsored by an industry or other organisations.

DEP408.desc: The Design Research Project involves student researching in an area related to design and is expected to produce new insightson the topic. This will involve in-depth analysis of an area that is of interest to Design. The work may involve primary and secondary research, framing research questions, creative exploration of alternatives,choosing a suitable methodology for analysis, experimental set-ups and methodical documentation. The students areencouraged to make use of research methods for documentation analysis and experimentation. Use of photography, sketching, audio, video for research documentation purposes. The documented material is analyzed forinferences and insights. Students are expected to explore new fields, materials and media, with a focus on analysis. Students need to choose a topic in consultation with a faculty member and work under faculty guidance.

DH 307.desc:
The DH307  Research and Development Project course offers students a unique opportunity to undertake hands-on research and development in
the field of Digital Health under the mentorship of faculty members associated with KCDH, IIT Bombay. Projects will focus on 6-7 research areas.
Students are expected to contribute meaningfully to an research project and/or development work, gaining practical exposure to various methods,
tools, and problem-solving strategies. Evaluation is based on contribution to the project, project reports, presentations, and final deliverables as
decided by the respective faculty mentor.
Procedure for Students to opt for DH307 (R&D Project Course):
1. View Project Topics:
a. The list of available projects, along with brief descriptions and prerequisites, will be published at the following link: Project List  DH307
https://docs.google.com/document/d/12b5IOzmQIreUDkhrW5m8QSQbT4up2qtFERY3iUtSzQ/edit?usp=sharing)
2. Submit Preferences:
a. Students interested in registering must fill out the DH307
Application Form (https://forms.gle/LXxS9yzV5TuLUU4W)
b. Required documents:
i. Statement of Purpose (SOP) explaining your interest
in the selected projects.
ii. Updated CV.
c. Students may indicate up to five project preferences:
i. Preferences 1: Compulsory
ii. Preferences 2 to 5: Optional
ii.iii. Note: Your application will be shared only with the faculty whose projects you have given preference to.
d. Application Deadline: 15th July 2025
3. Faculty Interaction and Selection:
a. Based on applications, faculty may:
i. Connect with students for interviews or share
evaluation tasks, or
ii. Directly select students based on application
material.
b. Upon final selection, the faculty will sign a consent form for
the student.
4. Consent Form and Final Registration:
a. Selected students must:
i. Fill the registration form
(https://forms.gle/gwTASJ3cYjnx68wf9 ).
ii. Upload the signed consent form with project details.
b. Only students with valid consent forms will be permitted to
register during the institute`s course registration window.
c. Course TA will maintain and update a daily list of approved
students for final registration clearance.
5. DX Grade Policy:
a. Students not demonstrating active contribution to the assigned project may be awarded a DX grade, based on the discretion of the faculty mentor.
For any queries, students may contact the the KCDH office. Further updates will be shared via e-mail on student mailing list.

DH 801.desc:
1) Statistical methods for understanding and interpreting biological and public health data.
2) Descriptive Statistics, Screening tests, ROC curves, prevalence and incidence of disease.
3) Application of regression to biological and health data sets: graphical data summary,
statistical comparison of interventions by hypothesis tests and confidence intervals, correlation,
and regression. Case studies.
4) Design and Analysis techniques for Epidemiologic Studies: Study designs, confounding and
standardization, Meta analysis, Analysis of variance and covariance. crossover designs, clustered
data, intraclass correlation, longitudinal analysis, missing data, case studies
5) Categorical health data: Contingency tables, Goodness of fit tests, Tests for binomial
proportions, estimation of power and sample size, logistic regression, case studies
6) Person time health data: Inference for incidence rate data, power and sample size considerations, testing for trend, survival analysis, proportional hazard model, case studies
7) Case studies of health big data: techniques to summarize and display big data, descriptive and
inferential methodologies
8) Clinical trials: statistical design and analysis aspects of clinical trials

Section 3: Technologies in Wearable HealthInternet of Things, Cloud computing and Telemedicine systemsArtificial Intelligence & Machine Learning in Wearable TechnologyFramework for integration with digital networksSection 4: Data Validation and Management in Wearables DevicesPotential interference factors that affect the reliability of wearable sensors.Data standards for interoperabilityPrivacy, Security & EthicsSection 5: Applications: Current and FuturisticHealthcare monitoring embedded in clothing and wearable accessories.Virtual Reality and Augmented Reality in Wearable Healthcare devices; Health Gamification. Non-conventional Applications: Sports medicine, Space Health, Mental Health, Oral Health, Defence

DS 203.desc:
Programming Basics (Python
programming, R, Data Structures),
Visualization/Plotting, Data Science
Libraries (Pandas, PyPlot, matplotlib)
Databases, GPUs/CUDA programming,
Parallel/distributed computing for data
science (Map/Reduce, Spark/Hadoop),
working on the cloud (Amazon Web
services, Google Cloud Platform, Azure,
etc). The course will be programming
heavy, with i n-class and take-home
programming exercises A project can be
