A mini-project would involve a combination of about 4-5 methods from above into solving multiple aspects of a single  problem (For e.g: Determining residual stress during cooling of a 1-D rod, and determining isocontours, Generation of such data and optimization of process parameters etc)

ME 899.desc: Context of communication: Recognizing our capability androles as professionalsScientific Method: Question and answer aspects oftechnical communication; Scientific Methodology and itsrelationship to technical communication;Surveying literature: Categories; reading and organizingscientific literature; search engines and tools.Listening and Note taking: 5-R method and mind-mapping.Technical writing: Report organization; Journal selection;Introduction, conclusion, and abstract writing.Speaking & Presentation skills: Organization ofpresentation slides (number, content, and formatting); Oralpresentations; Audience/context dependent practices; Nonverbal aspects: body language, eye-contact, personalappearance, facing large audience.Elevator pitch: Pitches for technical audience and policymakersWorkplace communication: Sensitivity towards genderand diversity; Email communication and netiquettes.Ethics in academic communication: Intellectual Property,copyrights and plagiarism; Authorship; Data ethics; Biasesand balanced criticism of literature;Suggested additional topics relevant to disciplines: Datarepresentation, Group discussion and interviews; accessiblescientific writing, report writing using LaTeX, Proofreading, etc

ME7002.desc:
* Fundamentals of Production Systems: Types of production systems: job shop, batch, cellular, flow, and continuous; System design principles and performance metrics; Capacity planning, line balancing, and facility layout; Production flow analysis and bottleneck identification

     * Manufacturing Planning and Control: Forecasting, aggregate planning, and demand management; Inventory systems; Scheduling and sequencing for flow and job-shop environments;

     * Quality and Reliability Engineering: Statistical process control (SPC) and process capability; Six Sigma methodologies and design of experiments; Reliability, maintainability, and asset health management; Total Productive Maintenance (TPM)

     * Introduction to Smart Manufacturing: Cyber-Physical Production Systems (CPPS); Industrial loT, data acquisition, and real-time monitoring; Digital twins for process optimization; Simulation of manufacturing systems; Multi-objective optimization: cost, productivity, energy; Decision support systems and advanced analytics

     * Sustainable Manufacturing Practices: Resource efficiency: energy, materials, and water; Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) fundamentals; Sustainable product and process design

     * Circular and Resilient Production Systems: Concepts of circularity: remanufacturing, reuse, recycling; Designing production systems for flexibility and resilience; Carbon footprint reduction strategies in production; Industry best practices and case studies

ME6119.desc: Review of single DOF system vibrations; Lagrange,s equation of motion; Multi DOF system vibration - Different methods to form equations of motion and to find natural frequencies and mode shapes; Vibrations of continuous systems- Axial, torsional and lateral vibration of bars; Introduction to nonlinear and random vibration     Concept of feedback, open and closed loop systems. Review Laplace transforms. Transfer function. Concept of stability, poles, and zeros. Standard transient responses. Methods for synthesis and analysis of control: P, PD, PID control. Frequency domain techniques for control design. Examples of active vibration control.  Introduction to signals and sampling, Shannon sampling theorem. Control implementation using microcontroller.

ME6102.desc:
Introduction, applications of mechatronic systems. Elements of mechatronic system: Sensor,
actuator, plant, and controller. Hands-on experience with CDROM drive, scanner, or similar such system/s. 

Integrated mechanical-electronic design philosophy. Integrated analysis tools and examples, selection of sensors, actuators and drive systems. 

Microcontrollers for mechatronics: interfacing, programming, selection of microcontroller. UI and
software requirements. 

Control design in linear and nonlinear domains for rigid body and compliant motion systems,
control implementation: various techniques; practical issues in implementation: friction, backlash,
noise; digital control fundamentals. 

Signal processing for mechatronics and Fast Fourier Transform (FFT), analog and digital filters,
signal processing for nonlinear control. 

Advanced topics in mechatronics: Example:Micro-Mechatronic, Opto-mechatronics.

ME6110.desc:
The complete course contents along with (actual hours) and [cumulative hours] are shown below:

Overview of the course: scope and importance (1) [1]
Microfabrication Technologies: Capabilitiesand Limitations(2) [3]
Nanofabrication by Photons (9) [12]
Optical Lithography Overview and Limitations
Resolution Enhancement Techniques
Lithographic Techniques for Nanofabrication
Near-field Lithography and Interference Lithography
Two-photon Lithography
Nanofabrication by Charged Beams (9) [21]
E-beam Lithography: 
System and Principles of Electron Matter Interaction
Resolution Issues
Focused Ion Beam (FIB) Nano patterning: 
FIB System and Principles of Ion Matter Interaction
FIB Milling and Deposition, Resolution Issues
FIB/SEM Dual Beam Capabilities
Nanofabrication by Replication (5) [26]
Introduction to Nano Imprint Lithography (NIL)
Roll-to-roll (R2R) processingon a roll of ultrathin plastic or metal
Metrology at Nanoscale (2) [28]
Introduction to SPM, AFM and NSOM
Nanofabrication by Scanning Probes (5) [33]
AFM, NSOM and STM based Fabrication Techniques
Self-Assembly (2) [35]
Case Studies / Emerging Applications (3) [38]

ME6107.desc: 1. Introduction to groups: Definition of a group; Multiplication Table; Subgroups, cosets and classes; Homomorphisms and isomorphisms; Algebra of group; Lattice and vector spaces     2. Point and Space Groups: Symmetry of crystal lattices; Derivation of space groups; representation of point and translation groups     3. Irreducible representations: Reducibility; Orthogonality relations; Group characters; Regular representations; Reduction of representations; Projection Operators     4. Applications: Involving algebraic forms; Involving functions and operators; Involving tensors and tensor operators     5. Application in mechanics: Space-Time Symmetries; Conservation Laws; Application in theory of vibrations     6.  Symmetry operations in Fullerenes, Carbon Nanotubes etc     7.  Introduction to group theoretic bifurcation theory: Instability, wrinkling, Liapunov-Schmidt Reduction, Symmetry of solutions

MG 401.desc: Broad overview of marketing:: Nature & Scope Core Marketing Concepts and TasksMarket Environment scanning  Consumer Behavior and customer valueMarket Segmentation, Targeting and Positioning, Product and Brand concepts Pricing decisions Marketing Channels : Distribution and direct marketing Marketing communication and Promotion  Personal SellingMarketing Information System, Sales ForecastingAnd marketing Research.

MG 402.desc:
HRM as an emerging field of study
Emergence of the concept of HRM
Historical developments
Introducing concepts like engineering management, industrial management, personnel management and HRM
Role of HRM in organizational performance
Human resource planning
Recruitment and selection at workplace
Effective interviewing
Organizational orientation and induction
Performance Appraisal
Training and development
Future of HRM

MM 329.desc: Basics of Phase diagrams: Gibbs free energy 302226 composition diagrams; introduction to ternary phase diagrams. Diffusion: steady vs non-steady states, driving force and mechanisms, Fick302222s first and second laws. Interstitial and substitutional diffusion, high diffusivity paths. Introduction to interfaces: solid-vapour interface, grain and phase boundaries, coherent and incoherent interfaces, ledge growth, rate controlling steps, Gibbs-Thompson effect. Phase Transformations: Basics of nucleation and growth in diffusional transformations; devitrification in glass-ceramics; Johnson-Mehl-Avrami-Kolmogorov analysis; precipitation in Al-alloys and alumina-magnesia ceramics; pearlite and bainite transformations in steels; martensitic transformation in steels and zirconia ceramics; order-disorder transformations in Cu-Zn and Au-Cu alloys; spinodal decomposition in metals, silicate glasses; glass transition in inorganic glasses; phase transitions in ferroelectric ceramics.

MM 223.desc:
Basic terminologies. Simple thermodynamic systems (Hydrostatic system, Electrochemical Cell, Dielectric Slab, Paramagnetic Rod). 
Zeroth Law and Thermal Equilibrium. Thermometers and measurement of temperature. 
Concept of work for simple systems (P-V work, stretched wire, polarization, magnetisation, moving charge). Differential Form of the First Law. Heat Capacity and its measurement. Adiabatic Flame Temperature.
Concept of reversible and irreversible processes. Kelvin-Planck and Clausius statements of the Second Law. Statistical interpretation of temperature and entropy. Thermodynamic potentials.
Mathematical methods: Reciprocal Theorem, Triple Product Rule, Maxwells relations.
Third Law and its experimental verification.
Open systems and the concept of chemical potential.
Properties of ideal solutions and regular solutions. Gibbs-Duhem equation and Clausius-Clayperon equation.
Phase Equilibrium in a One-Component system. Phase Rule and Gibbs energy-composition diagrams. Simple binary systems.
Introduction to thermodynamic software - Thermo-Calc and FactSage Educational versions.

MM 221.desc:
Op-amps revision and introduction to LTspice simulation package for circuit design
Active components including diodes, transistors and optically active components
Passive components such as resistors and capacitors
Sensors such as temperature sensors, pressure sensors, photodiodes
Closed-loop control and simulation using Scilab Xcos

Experiments:

Concepts of measurement, calibration and errors
Passive components: fabrication and electrical characterization
Active optoelectronic components: electrical characterization and correlation with material properties. Utilizing these components in simple circuits for various applications.
Sensors: fabrication and calibration of sensors, for example, for temperature and pressure measurement
Closed-loop control and data acquisition.

Lab project comprising of the above components.

MM 333.desc:
Review of periodicity and symmetry of crystals, Reciprocal lattice. 

Brillouin zones, reflection condition and Braggs law, Laues condition for diffraction.

Kronig-Penney model, Band theory of solids, Energy bands, energy-wavevector diagrams, Density of states, Fermi-Dirac distribution function. Free electron theory of metals

Semiconductors: Carrier concentration, effective mass, band gap measurement, intrinsic and extrinsic semiconductors. Doping and carrier concentration.

Dielectric and Optical properties- polarization, types and mechanisms, macroscopic and local fields, polarizability. Dispersion and complex dielectric constant. Complex refractive index, Transmission, Reflection, Absorption and Plasma resonance.

Magnetic Properties - Paramagnetism, Curie-Weiss law, Pauli paramagnetism, molecular field theory, exchange interactions, ferro-, anti-ferro and ferri-magnetism, soft and hard magnetic materials, magnetization. Magnetoresistance.

Thermal properties - thermal conductivity, thermal expansion, specific heat.

Ferroelectric, piezoelectric, pyroelectric properties

MM 341.desc: Lecture:    302225 Theory of film deposition 302226 introduction to gauges, importance of vacuum in thin film deposition, principle of evaporation and sputtering and their variations.    302225 Sol-gel and oxidation processes    302225 Sheet resistance measurement and thickness effect, Hall effect, UV-Vis spectroscopy    302225 Aspects of XRD and SEM/XPS which are specific to thin films Experiments:    302225 1. Thermal evaporation of metal films (Al/Cu):Measure thickness, sheet resistance as a function of thickness for resistivity, Hall measurement for carrier concentration, XRD for phase and reflectance    302225 2. Reactive DC sputtering for TiN coating:Measure thickness, sheet resistance as a function of thickness for resistivity, Optical response-reflectance, nanoindentation for hardness.    302225 3. RF sputtering of Al2O3:Measure thickness, optical reflectance, dielectric breakdown as a function of thickness.    302225 4. Sol-get and spin coating of TiO2Morphology-optical and/or SEM, UV-Vis spectroscopy-Tauc plot, Construct a dye sensitized solar cell with mesoporous TiO2 and check power output     302225 5. E-beam evaporation of Sn and oxidation of SnO2 Measure thickness as a function of oxidation time, sheet resistance, Hall measurements for carrier concentration, mobility and type of carrier

MM 331.desc:
Mechanics Concepts: Axially loaded members: axial force,
Bending and shearing stresses in beams: types of beams, loads and reactions, shear and bending moment diagrams of beams;
Torsional deformation
Stress tensor
Strain tensor
Plane stress and strain, principal stresses and strains, Stress transformation (Mohr`s circle)
Hookes Law and anisotropic elasticity, 
Isostress and isostrain (composites).
Cohesive strength and theoretical shear strength of materials;
Types of dislocations (edge, screw and mixed), stress fields, properties and motion of dislocations, dislocation interactions; partials and twinning
Plastic deformation in single crystals - slip systems, resolved shear stress
Strengthening of materials: work hardening, grain boundary strengthening, solid solution strengthening, precipitation hardening, dispersion strengthening.
Fracture: Fracture modes, fracture mechanics, Ductile-to-brittle transition phenomenon, toughening mechanisms.
Deformation response of materials under cyclic loading conditions (fatigue): stress-life approach, strain-life approach and fracture mechanics approach, safe-life and fail-safe design criterion.
Deformation response of materials at elevated temperatures: creep curve, creep mechanisms, deformation mechanism maps.

MM 335.desc:
XRay diffraction theory - X Ray source, Bragg`s law, Phase identification, determining lattice parameter, significance of peak broadening (FWHM) and peak shift
Basics of microscopy - optical, electron microscopy, etching, resolution. Tutorial to teach quantitative metallography principles: Grain size and volume fraction, statistics and error on micrographs of different standard samples (steels, cast iron, brasses-bronzes, Al alloys). 
Hardness and tensile test measurement principles, compliance correction and data plotting and extraction
Impact test, fatigue test and creep test measurement principles

Experiments:
1. Casting - Induction melting OR Centrifugal casting OR ESR OR Sand casting of steels. Microstructure characterization for defects and inclusions. Density through Archimedes principle. 

2. Weldability (TIG) versus hardenability (Jominy End Quench) tests of a mild steel versus medium carbon steel OR Diffusion bonding of two similar or dissimilar non-ferrous metals in Gleeble OR Soldering Pd-Sn alloys. Defect characterisation using Dye Penetrant Test; Charpy Impact Test (weld/joining)/Creep (Solders). Microstructural characterization of welds

3. Cold rolling (work hardening) and annealing of non-ferrous metal/alloy OR Solutionizing, quenching and aging of an Al alloy;
XRD/Hardness and Resistivity measurements 
           Mechanical behavior through tensile testing to determine changes in yield strength and strain hardening behavior

4. Surface hardening treatments on steels-carburizing/grinding/shot peening; Hardness profile;
Impact on low cycle fatigue life

MM 451.desc: Modelling in mechanical translation, rotary and simple hydraulic and pneumatic systems, one tank and two tank process, Block diagrams, transfer functions and steady state behaviour of systems.  Dynamic behaviour, transient response and analysis, stability criterion.  Types of controllers on-off, P, PI, PID controllers.  Analog computers and their use in simulation and analysis of control systems.  Characteristics of measurements, temperature measurement, flow measurement, pressure measurement and level measurement.  Measurement of force and torque.  Measurement of acceleration.  P & ID of typical process case examples in instrumentation from metallurgical processes.

MM 474.desc:
A. Vacuum components and systems : Need for vacuum, ways to achieve vacuum, determination of vacuum, dry and vapour pumps, pressure measurement gauges, conductance and other system design considerations.

B. Thin film deposition techniques:  Physical and chemical vapour deposition techniques including molecular beam epitaxy, laser ablation and hot wire and microwave CVD techniques. Film contamination, cosine law of deposition, conformal coverage and line of sight deposition.

C. Growth of thin films: Thermodynamic and kinetic considerations of deposition of thin films by both CVD and PVD.  In situ characterization of thin film deposition process.

D. Characterization of thin films:  Different methods of thickness measurements, electrical, optical, chemical and structural property determination.

E. Some important applications of thin films: Hard and decorative coatings, semiconductor thin films, organic thin films.

MM 409.desc: Natures of interfaces, Thermodynamics of interfaces, Adsorption and deposition at surfaces and interfaces, Surface tension, Capillarity and wetting, Surfactants, Micelles, Critical micelle concentration, Emulsions, Surface activity in emulsions, Stability of emulsions, Demulsification, Foams, Foam structures, Foam drainage and stability, Foam inhibition and antifoams. Measurement of surface and interfacial tension, Measurement of contact angle, Fine particles, Colloid materials, and colloid stability, Clusters, Nanomaterials, Light scattering by small particles, Self-organization, Interfacial forces and fields, Electrical double layer, Hamaker constant, DLVO theory, vander-waals forces, Electrostatic, steric and electrosteric stabilization, Zeta potential, Flocculation, Coagulation, Electrokinetics, and Rheology of dispersions, Porous materials and membranes, Thin films, Surfactant assisted nanoparticle synthesis, Applications of colloid and interfacial science.

MM 611.desc: Experiments involving processing, structural characterization and property evaluation of steel.  The topics that would be covered are : Processing: melting and casting, heat treatment, thermo-mechanical processing, sheet metal forming and welding. Structural characterization: phase transformation, electron microscopy, crystallography by using modern techniques. Properties: mechanical properties (like fatigue, fracture toughness, integrity assessment) relating to structure and processing, corrosion evaluation.

MM 641.desc:
Review of basic numerical methods like root finding, numerical integration, solving systems of equations, multiple linear regression etc. with special emphasis on computer implementation and applications in material processing. Initial and boundary value problems, partial differential equations. Introduction to finite difference (FDM) and finite element (FEM) methods. 
Applications in materials processing : heat transfer and solidification; metal forming processes : analysis of sheet necking, formability tests, forming limit diagrams (FLD) etc.

MM 621.desc: Microstructure & Properties: solidification and solidification structures, interfaces, crystallographic texture, residual stress, structure-property relations. Plasticity and work hardening: fundamentals, stress-strain behavior, fracture, creep & deformation mechanisms. Recovery, recrystallization, grain growth.  Phase transformation: thermodynamic basics, nucleation and growth, spinodal decomposition, martensitic transformations

MM 612.desc: The lab involves the development and code implementation of numerical methods for solving different metallurgical and materials engineering problems. Topics covered include : i) Preliminaries of programming: Operations with arrays, conditional statements, reading data from files and manipulation, plotting data in 2D and 3D ii) Root finding: Newton-Raphson, bisection, secant methods.      iii) Matrix algebra: Solution of system of equations, Gauss-Jordan and Gauss-Siedel methods.       iv) Eigen value analysis: Eigen value and vectors of matrices.       v) Least squares regression: Linear and multi-variate regression.       vi) Interpolation: Polynomial interpolation, Lagrange interpolating polynomials.      vii) Numerical differentiation: First order and second order derivatives using forward, backward and central finite divided differences.        viii) Numerical integration: Trapezoidal rule, Simpson`s 1/3rd and 3/8th rules.

MM 651.desc: Simple and composite systems, stable equilibrium states.  Adiabatic work interaction, heat interaction, internal energy, First law.  Reversible processes, heat engines, Second law, Theorem of Clausius, entropy, combined first and second law.  Legendre transforms, representations of the fundamental equation.  Equilibrium: extremum principles, membrane, phase and reaction equilibria.  Single phase systems: Thermodynamic relations among variables.  Solutions, partial molal properties, solution models.  Phase rule, unary, binary and ternary phase diagrams.  Thermodynamics of phase diagrams.  Reacting systems, gas phase reactions, Ellingham diagrams, Pourbaix diagrams.

MM 654.desc: Principles of composites, micromechanics of composites.  Various types of reinforcements and their properties.  Role of interfaces.  Fabrication of metal matrix composites : insitu, dispersion hardened, particle, whisker and fibre reinforced; composite coatings by electrodeposition and spray forming.  Fabrication of polymeric and ceramic matrix composites.  Mechanical physical properties of composites.  Mechanisms of fracture in composites.  Property evaluation and NDT of composites.  Wear and environmental effects in composites.

MM 659.desc: Review of fundamentals in fluid flow, heat transfer and mass transfer.  Introduction of turbulent flow phenomena.  Heat transfer with phase change.  A few detailed examples of transport phenomena applications to materials processing.

MM 681.desc:
Molecular theory of deformation kinetics. Rate theory of plastic deformation.  Micormechanistic approach for deformation behaviour of single crystals and polycrystals.  Low temperature deformation of metals and other crystalline colids. Dynamic strain aging, creep, internal stress.
Deformation of intermetallic compounds.
Substructural evolution at large strains.  Recovery, recrystallization and grain growth during high temperature deformation. Formation cells boundaries, slip in a cell structure and composite model of time dependent flow.

MM 685.desc: 1. Postulates of quantum mechanics, Bra Ket notation, Particles in box, Simple harmonic oscillator, Quantum free electron theory, Fermi Dirac statistics, Density of States, Fermi gas, Boltzmann Transport Equation, Electrical properties in metal.     2. Concepts of Reciprocal space and symetry, Laue Condition of diffraction, Brillouin zone, Band Theory of Solid, electron in periodic potentiaols, Bloch`s theorem, Kronig Penny model and solutions, E-k diagrams, effective mass.     3. Introduction to phenon, Phenon modes in monoatomic and diatomic 1D chain.     4. Direct and indirect semiconductors, Intrinsic and Extrinsic semiconductors, Fermi level, Carrier conentration, Hall effect in semiconductors, Generation and recombination from Boltzmann Transport equation.     5. Dielectric and optical properties of semiconductor and indulator, Dielectric constant, polarizability, Sources of polarizability- dipolar, iconic, electronic, Complex dielectric constant, dielectric losses.     6. Introduction to magnetic materials : Diamagnetism, Paramagnetism amd Ferromagnetism, Spin and angular momentum, Hund`s rule, Quantum theory of magnetism, Exchange mechanism.

MM 699.desc: Metallographic sample preparation method, Equilibrium potential measurement of Cu, galvanic potential measurement of Fe and Zn, corrosion rate by weight loss method, determination of electrochemical kinetic parameters (Tafel extrapolation) and demonstration of passivity by potentiodynamic polarization, intergranular corrosion tests: by ASTM A 262 A and EPR techniques; Electrochemical impedance technique and study of corrosion inhibitors, Coating preparation by spray method and coating characterization (DFT, Impact), Surface energy measurement of coating using contact angle measurement. High temperature corrosion, and demonstration of stress corrosion cracking.

MM 695.desc: Low temperature vs high temperature corrosion. Introduction to oxidation, thermodynamics of gas/metal reaction, stability of oxides/sulphides. Ellingham diagrams; oxidation kinetics 302226 linear, parabolic, cubic, logarithmic rate laws, Wagner302222s theory of oxidation. Types of oxidation: general oxidation, selective oxidation, internal oxidation, breakaway and catastrophic oxidation; defects in oxides (p&n type), Wagner Hauffe Rules, Kroger Vink notations; oxidation vs sulphidation, hot-corrosion, oxidation of important metals and alloys; Use of electron-optical techniques in assessing oxidation damage and understanding oxidation mechanism. Practical examples of high temperature oxidation 302226 power plants, gas turbines, petrochemical plants etc.

MM 713.desc:
Importance of corrosion, Corrosion loss, beneficial use of corrosion, Aqueous corrosion, Electrochemical cell, EMF, Galvanic series, Anatomy of a corrosion cell, Electrochemical thermodynamics, Equilibrium potential, Nernst equation and its application, Pourbaix diagram. Electrochemical kinetics, Corrosion rate expression, weight loss method, corrosion allowance, corrosion potential, OCP, exchange current density, electrochemical polarization and its types, Activation polarization: derivation of the Butler-Volmer equation, Tafel slopes, High and low field approximations, Linear polarization, Polarization resistance, Mixed potential theory, Butler-Volmer equation for multielectrode system, Evans diagram Diffusion effect in corrosion, Mass transport polarization; Resistance polarization; Passivity, Forms of corrosion, general corrosion: Galvanic corrosion: Crevice corrosion: Pitting: Intergranular corrosion: weld decay, knife line attack, ASTM standard test; Stress corrosion Cracking (SCC): ; Hydrogen damage: Corrosion fatigue, Tribocorrosion, Corrosion in concrete, Filiform corrosion, Erosion-Corrosion & Cavitation, Dealloying; Atmospheric corrosion: environmental factors, classification of atmosphere, effect of pollution, ISO classification of corrosivity, Corrosion in fresh water, Corrosion in soil; Corrosion in renewable energy systems. Aqueous Corrosion in various industry situation.

Corrosion prevention methods: Cathodic protection (CP), Introduction to Coatings; Alloy design for corrosion mitigation, Materials selection for corrosion. Course project.

MM 658.desc:
Theory:
Thermodynamics of fracture-Griffith theory
Stability of cracks, Crack resistance curves
Linear elastic fracture mechanics, Irwin and Dugdale plastic zone correction 
Standards and testing methods to measure stress intensity factor
Ductile failure, J-integral and CTOD
Fracture mechanisms in metals
Toughening mechanisms in brittle materials and composites
Fracture mechanics of layered/graded structures, and thin films and coatings
Fail-safe design and damage tolerance concepts
Failure Analysis in terms of fatigue, embrittlement, SCC, creep
Reliability Evaluation and Life Extension Design
Practicals: 
1. Measure fracture toughness of a metallic bulk sample using LEFM, EPFM and COD (using DIC).
2. Demonstrate difference between notch (root radius) and pre-crack
3. Demonstrate size (thickness) dependence of fracture toughness and breakdown of plane strain conditions-any one geometry can be used
4. Demonstrate geometry independence of fracture toughness: Single cantilever bending, Double Cantilever bending or CT, Single edge notch bending (in 3 and 4 point), Clamped beam bending, Double edge notched tension
5. Evaluate mode II/mode I ratios in 3 point, 4 point bending-eccentric loading and angled notches and in CT/tension specimen using angled notches
6. Demonstrate toughening mechanism by 3-D printing 2 phase polymers or architectured samples
7. Interface fracture testing: 4 point bending of laser cut vs 3D printed vs glued samples

MM 729.desc: Introduction to statistical methods for large corrosion data analysis Corrosion fundamentals and characterization techniques, Electrochemical techniques for corrosion monitoring, Electrochemical polarization techniques, Electrochemical noise, Electrochemical Impedance spectroscopy, Gravimetric techniques, Electrical resistance techniques, Sensors for corrosion monitoring, Cathodic protection monitoring, Localized corrosion monitoring methods - SECM, SVET, drop cell, SCC measurement techniques, Surface analytical techniques. Introduction to non-destructive techniques.

MM 719.desc: Pair potentials, Variational principles,  Born  Oppenheimer Approximation,  Hartree Approximation, Hartree  Fock Approximation, , Introduction to Molecular Dynamics, Introduction to Density Functional Theory,  Case studies

MM 730.desc: 1. Concepts of stress and strain - Force equilibrium, stress tensor, Deformation gradient, strain tensor.2. Elasticity - Anisotropic crystalline elasticity, Anistropic elasticity of composites (iso-stress and iso-strain), Viscoelasticity of polymers3. Plasticity of crystalline materials - Slip and twining in cubic and hexagonal close packed crystals, Dislocation mechanics, Strengthening mechanisms and work hardening in single crystals and polyscystalline materials, creep, fatigue, constitutive relations, yield criteria and surfaces.4. Plasticity of non-crystalline materials - Shear tranformation zones and shear banding in glassy materials.5. Fracture - Linear elastic fracture mechanics in brittle materials: concepts of energy relearse rate and stress intensity factor, Standard fracture toughness testing techniques and geometric factors, Fracture mechansims in metallic materials (ductile/brittle/intergranular) under environments, Toughening mechanisms in ceramics and ceramic matrix composites, Failure mechanisms in non-crystalline materials (crazing).

MM 722.desc:
Lecture and Tutorials: (Numbers are an indication of important topics, not of the number of lectures.)
1.Brief overview of statistical mechanics: Equilibrium and ergodicity, Time- and ensemble-averages, Partition function
2.Statistical ensembles
3.Molecular dynamics simulations: Basic algorithm, Initialization, Integration of equations of motion, Verlet algorithm, Neighbour-lists, Boundary conditions, Force-fields, Long-range interactions  Ewald sums, Particle mesh approach, Thermostats and barostats
4.Free energy calculation methods: Umbrella sampling, Thermodynamic perturbation
5.Brief introduction to coarse-grained simulation methods: Langevin equation, Langevin and Brownian dynamics method
6.Demonstration of ergodic hypothesis: 
7.Calculation of radial density distribution function and diffusivity
8.Implementation of a basic molecular dynamics algorithm
9.Implementation of free energy calculation methods: Examples of umbrella sampling and thermodynamic perturbation
10.Implementing molecular dynamics simulation using open-source packages (LAMMPS)
11.Examples from different materials systems emphasizing the role of microstructure in determining materials properties:
a.Radiation damage in metals
b.Calculation of grain boundary energies
c.Twin boundaries in metal deformation
d.Dispersion of nanoparticles (e.g. carbon nanotubes in water)
e.Dynamics o

MM 732.desc:
Optical microscopy  different modes of microscopy, image analysis
Scanning electron microscopy  secondary electron and backscattered electron imaging,
EDS/EPMA
X-ray diffraction  indexing of XRD patterns, Intensity calculations, particle size effects, lattice
parameter determination

MM 735.desc:
Importance of High temperature measurements-relevance to Materials Processing, Basic
principles of measurements at high temperature: Temperature measurement (thermocouples,
optical pyrometers), gas flow and composition, importance of gas cleaning, detection of trace
constituents (e.g. oxygen sensing), means to attain high temperature, requirement of special
atmosphere, choice of crucible/sample container, Thermal analysis: DTA, DSC, TGA,
dilatometry, Measurement of Thermodynamic parameters:EMF measurement, equilibration with
gas mixtures, iso-piestic method, special methods, Concept of capacity of a slag: e.g. sulphide,
phosphate, carbonate, alkali, etc., Thermophysical measurements:density, viscosity, interfacial
tension, thermal conductivity, Particle characterisation: pycnometry, BET analysis, porosimetry,
Case studies

MM 754.desc:
I. Lectures
     Introduction
        ◦ Emerging pollutants in water bodies
        ◦ In Situ Sampling Techniques 
        ◦ Sustainable Water Treatment Techniques
     Membrane-Based technologies
     Forward Osmosis, Electrospun Membranes, Membrane Distillation and Microbial Fuel Cell
     Bio and Bio-inspired materials for water reclamation
     Bioreactor for degradation, Bioremediation and Phytoremediation, 
     Hydrodynamic cavitation and Advanced Oxidation Process
     Nanomaterials for treatment of water

    II. Assignments: There will be regular assignments during which students learn to understand the theory

     The assessment for this course would be based on in-class participation, in-class short quizzes, mid-sem examination, assignments, and end-sem examination.

MM 747.desc: Course Content (List of the topics/sub302255topics to be covered in the lectures/practicals/assignments):Introduction to wave function based methods: Wave function, many body problem, Variational principles, Hartree and Hartree Fock methods, Introduction to density functional theory: Kohn Sham Equations, self consistency cycles, Exchange and Correlations, Basis set, and Pseudopotential, Application of Density functional theory based methods in materials science: Simulation, visualization, and optimization, Crystal structure, Cohesive energy and formation energy of solid, Electronic properties of solid, Vibrational properties of solid, Elastic properties of solid, Thermodynamic properties of solid, Defect formation energies in solid, Magnetic structure of solid, Molecular Dynamics.

MM 734.desc:
Conductivity measurements (bulk and thin films)  2 and 4 probe measurements in metals,
semiconductors and insulators. Hall effect, mobility, carrier concentration measurements.
AC measurements  impedance and dielectric. Various sources  grain, grain boundary, space
charge, electron. Activation energy. Optical measurements  band gap in semiconductors.
Absorption measurements, photoconductivity, photoluminescence for defects. Ellipsometry.
Work function measurement  UPS, metal-semiconductor contacts, Kelvin probe.
IR and Raman spectroscopy. Ferro-electric and ferro-magnetic measurements

MM 733.desc:
Axial (tension and compression) testing of materials. Torsion test. Bend test (bulk and thin
films). Hardness (Macro, Micro and Nano  Indentation). Scratch and wear test. Fracture (Impact
test). Fatigue test. Creep and superplasticity.

MM 762.desc: A processing overview highlighting the front-end of the line (FEOL) and back-end of the line (BEOL) processes involved in semiconductor device fabrication. Details of the following processing methods will be covered in this course:    1. Wafer fabrication by melt growth: sand to ingot to wafer, Czochralski and float zone method    2. Thermal oxidation and Si/SiO2 interface: Oxide growth kinetics, dependence of growth kinetics on processing parameters like temperature and pressure    3. Dopant diffusion: Ficks laws of diffusion. Analytical and numerical solutions. Diffusion in semi-infinite, finite and composite media, Atomistic models of diffusion. Diffusion mechanisms. Diffusion profile calculations    4. Thin film deposition:        * Thermodynamics of nucleation and growth. Growth modes based on energetic considerations, epitaxy, substrate requirements for epitaxy, strain considerations, dislocations due to lattice mismatch, formation of thick films for substrates and films as the thin active layer.        * Thin film deposition techniques (PVD): Thornton`s diagram, Thermal evaporation, Sputter deposition (DC, RF, pulsed DC), MBE, Pulsed Laser Deposition, e-beam evaporation        * Thin film deposition techniques (CVD): boundary layer formation, gas phase diffusion profiles of precursors, gas phase reactions, surface reaction and surface diffusion rates, types of CVD: MOCVD, APCVD, ALD, etc.    5. Capstone project: The project will include evaluating the instrumentation, parameter window, advantages and disadvantages of growth methods like MOCVD, APCVD, ALD, MBE, HVPE, sputter deposition, pulsed laser deposition, etc.

MM 755.desc: (List of the topics/sub-topics to be covered in the /ectures,/pract;rrtr/assiennents):. Kinetics fundamental  (2 weeks). Reaction kinetics Rate of interfacial reactions between fluidsD iffusion fundamentaIs" li,lass transfer-control led reactions Interfacial phenomena and its role in process metallurgy (l week). Surface tension models. Interfacial convection" Appl ications in process metal lurgyHeterogeneous reactions with examples (2 weeks):" Solid-liquid, sol id-gas, I iquid_l iquid, I iquid_gasDissolution of additives in the mert, Reduction reactions,and Degassing or gas pick-up in Iiquid metalsMultistep reactions (2 weeks)" Mass transfer and interfacial" Identifying rate control I ing. lvlixed control reactionsSlagrnetal reactions,reaction-control led reactionsstepsStirring of morten metars/srags and its kinetic effects (2 weeks). Formation and role of bubbles. Gas stirred ladles. Foaming, Electromagnetic stirring

MM 759.desc: Part I: Structure (Pre-midsem)          1) Lattice Geometry - Unit cell, lattice planes and directions, Weiss zone law, symmetry elements (translational, rotational, reflection), restrictions on symmetry elements, crystal systems and Bravais lattices.          2) Point Groups and Space Groups - Macroscopic symmetry elements, Orthorhombic, tetragonal, cubic, hexagonal, trigonal, monoclinic and triclinic systems.          3) Crystal Structures - Common metallic structures: cubic close packed, hexagonal close packed, double hexagonal close packed, body centered cubic Diamond, graphite, hexagonal boron nitride. Simple MX and MX2 compounds. Inorganic compounds: perovskite, spinel, inverse spinel, garnet, calcite, alpha-alumina,Intermetallic compounds and ordered structures.          4) Directions in the crystal lattice; distances and angles in a 3-D lattice; lattice planes and Miller indices; Metric Tensor. Reciprocal space; reciprocal metric tensor. Stereographic projection, zones, zone axes, relationship between direct and reciprocal lattice, coordinate transformations.          5) Amorphous Materials and Special Types of Crystal - Solid Aggregates. Liquid crystals, geometry of polyhedra, icosahedral packing, quasi-crystals, incommensurate structures, foams, porous and cellular materials.          6) Tensors - Tensors: definition, coordinate transformations, dummy index notation, principal axes, crystal symmetry and property tensors, field tensors, representation quadric, second, third and fourth rank tensors.          Part II: Defects (Post-midsem)          7) Point Defects - Point defects in ionic crystals and ceramics (defect classification, Kroger-Vink Notation, Defect reactions, Defect equilibria and Kroger-Vink diagrams), point defect aggregates, point defect configurations, point defects in equilibrium, radiation damage, anelasticity and point defect symmetry.          8) Dislocations - Dislocation motion, force on a dislocation, distortion in a dislocated crystal. Dislocation-dislocation interactions, atom positions close to a dislocation.          9) Dislocations in Crystals - Strain energy of a dislocation, partials and stacking faults, dislocations in ccp and hexagonal metals, dislocations in bcc crystals, dislocation in rock salt structure, dislocations in covalent solids.         10) Stacking Faults and Twinning - Stacking faults, deformation twinning, examples of twin structures in CCP, BCC and HCP metals, twinning in zinc blende, calcite and graphite, twinning elements, morphology of deformation twinning.         11) Grain Boundaries, Texture and interphase boundaries - Structure and energy of grain boundaries, interface junctions, structure and energy of surfaces, shapes of crystals and grains, interphase boundary, strained layer epitaxy.

MM 768.desc:
1. Thermal evaporation of metal films (Al/Cu):Measure thickness, sheet resistance as a function of thickness for resistivity, Hall measurement for carrier concentration, XRD for phase and reflectance.
      2. Reactive DC sputtering for TiN coating: Measure thickness, sheet resistance as a function of thickness for resistivity, Optical response-reflectance, nanoindentation for hardness.
      3. RF sputtering of Al203:Measure thickness, optical reflectance, dielectric breakdown as a function of thickness.
      4. Sol-gel and spin coating of TiO 2 Morphology-optical and/or SEM, UV-Vis spectroscopy-Tauc plot, Construct a dye sensitized solar cell with mesoporous TiO 2 and check power output
      5. Hot Injection method of synthesis of quantum dots, measure the UV-Vis spectroscoрy,
band gap and variation in band gap, measure photoluminescence and obtain the PL QY. Combine different materials and make a UV-pumped LED.
      6. 3D printing of polymers - fracture test. Epoxy-fiber composite with different amounts of filler loading. TG-DTA/DSC. Impact test as a function of temperature

MM 899.desc: Context of communication: Recognizing our capability androles as professionalsScientific Method: Question and answer aspects oftechnical communication; Scientific Methodology and itsrelationship to technical communication;Surveying literature: Categories; reading and organizingscientific literature; search engines and tools.Listening and Note taking: 5-R method and mind-mapping.Technical writing: Report organization; Journal selection;Introduction, conclusion, and abstract writing.Speaking & Presentation skills: Organization ofpresentation slides (number, content, and formatting); Oralpresentations; Audience/context dependent practices; Nonverbal aspects: body language, eye-contact, personalappearance, facing large audience.Elevator pitch: Pitches for technical audience and policymakersWorkplace communication: Sensitivity towards genderand diversity; Email communication and netiquettes.Ethics in academic communication: Intellectual Property,copyrights and plagiarism; Authorship; Data ethics; Biasesand balanced criticism of literature;Suggested additional topics relevant to disciplines: Datarepresentation, Group discussion and interviews; accessiblescientific writing, report writing using LaTeX, Proofreading, etc.

MM6102.desc:
* Basics of Materials Thermodynamics:
Laws of Thermodynamics, Gibbs energy and its relation to other thermodynamic quantities, Phase equilibria and phase diagrams, Unary, binary, and ternary phase diagrams, Solution thermodynamics

     * Introduction to Calphad (Calculation of Phase Diagrams)

     * Introduction to Thermo-Calc and FactSage thermodynamic software packages

     * Computational Methods to compute phase equilibria and phase diagrams

     * Thermodynamic Models for solution and compound phases

     * Experimental determination and ab initio calculations of thermodynamic quantities

     * Construction of Thermodynamic Databases based on experimental data

     * Application of Calphad method to materials design in different alloy systems like steels, Ni-based, Al, etc.

     The assessment for this course would be based on in-class participation, mid-sem, assignments using

     Thermo-Calc and FactSage, and end-sem.

MM6003.desc: Module I: Motivation and Foundations Microstructure Texture, Advanced Crystallography.Module II: Orientation and Texture Representation Pole Figures, Inverse Pole Figures, Orientation Distribution Function, Mathematical Formulation of ODF, Harmonic Series Expansion, ODF sections, Texture Indices.Module III: Mathematical Formulations Orientation and Texture.Module IV: Texture Measurement Techniques Bulk and Microtexture Measurements.Module V: Texture Evolution Mechanisms Deformation Texture, Annealing Texture, Texture through Other Processing.Module VI: Engineering with Texture Elastic Anisotropy, Plastic Anisotropy, Texture and Fatigue/Creep,Texture and Functional Properties, Modeling Texture Evolution,Texture Design

MM 772.desc:
*** Module I: Physical and Computational Simulation of Industrial Process Routes

     1. Systems Overview: Introduction to industrial production encompassing continuous casting. multi-stage hot rolling, cold reduction, and continuous annealing cycles.

     2. Laboratory Simulation: Application of Vacuum Induction Melting or alloy design, dilatometry for phase transformation Kinetics, and Gleeble-based thermomechanical processing for strain-path emulation.

     3. Kinetic Modeling: Analysis of experimental data utilizing the Johnson-Mehl-Avrami-Kolmogorov (JMAK) framework to quantify global kinetics of static/dynamic recrystallization and phase transformations.

     *** Module II: Divergence Between Laboratory Models and Industrial Realities

     1. The Critical Gap in Laboratory Experiments: Critical reassessment of the TTT diagrams for Steels. The invalidity of (even) the CCT diagrams for industrial processing. Fundamental (technical) limitations of experiments in replicating industrial processes

     2. Application of Avrami Equation for obtaining non-isothermal kinetics.

     3. The significant impact of Scheil`s additivity principle along with the heat of recalescence on altering phase transformation kinetics.

     4. The defining role of rate of heating and by implication the limitation of lab-based thermomechanical experiments on the Recrystallization kinetics of Steel

     *** Module III: Discussions on lack of simulation equipment for duplicating processes that impact important properties of steels - surface finish. Roll "Crown" in rolling mill, screw down mechanism, edgers and other equipment for removal of scales during hot rolling.

MM 763.desc:
Introduction to vacuum technology, gauge and pump
Experiments:
    * Thermal oxidation of silicon - effect of temperature, pressure and time on oxidation thickness and quality. Thickness and optical constant determination using spectroscopic ellipsometry. MOSCap analysis
    * Metal deposition by puttering and e-beam evaporation - thickness and microstructure correlation with pressure and power. Thickness and sheet resistance measurement
    * Oxide film deposition by reactive sputtering-property correlation with power and oxygen partial pressure, thickness, optical property determination and electrical characterization of MOScap using a shadow mask.

MM6103.desc:
Part 1: Preliminaries
Vector and tensor spaces, derivatives of tensor fields, integral theorems, continuum transport equation, conservation equations for mass and momentum.
Error analysis, numerical differentiation, trapezoidal rule, Simpsons rules, matrix and linear algebra (Gauss elimination, LU decomposition, Gauss-Seidel method, Eigen value and Eigen vector analysis), Lagrange polynomial interpolation.
Part 2: Numerical Solutions of Ordinary and Partial Differential Equations
Eulers and Heuns methods, Runge-Kutta methods, stiffness (implicit/explicit Euler), boundary value problem (finite difference and Eigen value problem), finite difference (explicit, implicit, Crank-Nicolson methods), finite element method.
Part 3: Modeling Transport Phenomena
Finite difference modeling of heat conduction and transfer, solidification, fluid flow and diffusion, finite element modeling of heat transfer.
Hands-on numerical implementation will be performed in MATLAB during the lectures.

MS 101.desc: 1. Engineering Drawing Basics: Projections, Sections, 3D Modeling interfaced with the software, Productassembly and tolerances, Manufacturing Practices (Conventional + Advanced)2. Kirchoff302222s current and voltage laws; independent and dependent sources. Passive electrical devices: R, L, C,and transformer. Electromechanical devices: electromagnetic relays, solenoids, DC motors,stepper motors. Electronic devices & circuits: pn junction diode, Zener diode, LED, photodiode, solar cell.Diode rectifiers: half-wave, full-wave, bridge; BJT and MOSFET amplifiers, BJT and MOSFET switches.Op amps, feedback amplifiers, oscillators, comparators, and Schmitt trigger.Logic gates and digital circuits, digital-to-analog converter (DAC), and analog-to-digital converter (ADC).Microprocessors, microcontrollers, memory and I/O devices.Sensors, actuators and interfacing.Microcontroller board with real-world interfacing.

MS 615.desc:
This course will expose the students to the fabrication techniques to fabricate real world products with different materials. The project-based lab course will cover the following content:   
 1. Precision machining using CNC turning and CNC milling, including parameter selection and quality evaluation.  
  2. Metal additive manufacturing covering process planning, build strategies, and post-processing steps.   
 3. Polymer injection molding with emphasis on mold design, processing conditions, and part quality control.   
 4. Composite and polymer processing using autoclave systems, including layup procedures and cure-cycle optimization.  
  5. Digital manufacturing workflows, including CAM integration, toolpath generation, and process monitoring.   
 6. Design for manufacturability across machining, additive manufacturing, molding, and composite fabrication.    
7. Inspection and metrology for dimensional accuracy, surface integrity, and defect identification.    8. Integrated prototyping involving fabrication, assembly, validation, and iterative design improvements.

MS 616.desc: The key objective of this course is for students to Design, Fabricate, Control & Demonstrate a palm top or table top sized robot (such as but not limited to drone, autonomous car, cart-pendulum etc.). This will include all associated aspects including Mechanical, Electrical and Algorithmic. Because of the background required, this course will have some pre-requisites on UG level Controls, Dynamics Theory Courses and Basic UG Level Electrical and Mechanical Labs.First half of the semester will have both prescribed modules and project work and second half will only have project work. During the first half semester each (three hour) lab session will have two hours of prescribed hand-on modules followed by one hour of project work. During the second half of the semester, full lab session will focus on the project work only.The hands-on prescribed module (total 7 sessions) will cover/recap key aspects of Robot Design and Control such as (1) Introduction Session (1 session): Overview of Course objective and activities to familiarize with Lab Hardware/Software, (2) Dynamics and Kinematics (2 sessions): Demonstrate principles using manipulator and/or drones, (3) Feedback Control (2 sessions): PID implementation exercise on a single motor, (4) Motion Planning (2 sessions): Motion planning exercise using a wheeled mobile robot.For the project, students will work in teams formed by the instructor randomly. Teams need to finalize their project within first few weeks under the supervision of the course instructor. Students need to brainstorm design ideas and work every week towards their project and will need to submit a short (max two pages) weekly report showing their work progress with key outputs in the week.

MS 614.desc:
This course will enable students to prototype and validate industry-grade product assembly systems by integrating automation using PLCs, sensors, and industrial communication. Mini projects emphasise hardware integration, control logic, sensing, mechanical interfacing, and system reliability in product assembly environments. The project-based lab course will cover the following content:

1. PLC-based control system design for assembly sequencing, interlocking, and      state-based operation.
2. Integration of industrial sensors including proximity, optical, force, vision, and environmental sensing for assembly feedback.
3. End-effector and actuator integration covering relay modules, motor starters, servo drives, pneumatic valves, and robot-PLC coordination.
4. Industrial communication networks including Modbus, Ethernet/IP, CANopen, Profinet, and OPC-UA for multi-device integration.
5. Real-time machine status acquisition using IO mapping, encoder feedback, load cells, smart sensors, and signal conditioning.
6. PLC programming using ladder logic (LD), structured text (ST), function block diagram (FBD), and sequential function charts (SFC) for assembly workflows.
7. Design and integration of HMI dashboards for operator control, alarms, and assembly diagnostics.
8. Sensor-driven error detection strategies including debounce logic, redundancy, voting mechanisms, and fault classification.

MS 618.desc: This course will contain projects that involve hands-on application of materials science and chemistry to real world problem statements, especially involving sustainable technologies such as green energy materials, recycling, and sustainable manufacturing. Depending on the faculty offering the course, projects may range from dye sensitized solar cells, quantum dots, wearable electronics, non-destructive sensor development for structural integrity assessment, recycling of E-waste, textile, packaging material, making feedstock for 3D printing, lifecycle assessment and recycling of structural alloys. These will be carried out in groups of 2-3, with a maximum cap of 20 students per semester. Interdisciplinary participation is highly encouraged.Dye sensitized solar cells, quantum dots, wearable electronics, non-destructive sensor development for structural integrity assessment,Electrodeposition will be used to extract critical metals from e-waste and redeposit them to make electrodes for batteries and other applications.Glass melting furnace (1400oC) will be used to melt waste glass, cast into shapes that can either be used directly or utilized further for glass blowing to form it into usable shapes or for aesthetic purposes.Mini extruder may be used to process shredded waste plastic to make filaments for 3D printing, Hot press will be used to recycle polymers and textiles with use of fillers into bricks / boards for structural, acoustic, thermal insulation applications. Different waste materials may first be shredded (if needed) to prepare them for further processing.Slip casting of ceramics/clays will be carried out to produce light weight ceramic products such as crucibles, trays, plates which may be used for high temperature applications or for making clay objects for various uses.Sheet metal work bench will be used to make sheet metal components with virgin sheet metal or to repurpose sheet metal from scrap.Induction melting will be used to melt waste metal into various shapes, prepare alloys and develop an understand the practise of foundry.All the above projects will use state of the art modelling techniques for alloy and chemistry design, while deploying hands-on skills of making the actual product, and will be coupled with sensors for diagnostics of structural health monitoring of the products made. The data collected will be used for improvements in design in the next iteration.

PH 114.desc: Thermal equilibrium, zeroth law and concept of temperature. First law and its consequences, reversible, irreversible and quasi-static processes. Second law: heat engines, concept of entropy and its statistical interpretation. Thermodynamic potentials, Maxwell`s relations. Joule Kelvin effect. Phase transitions, order of phase transitions, order parameter, critical exponents and the Clausius-Clapeyron equation. Applications to magnetism, superfluidity and superconductivity.

PH 109.desc:
1. Physics of Ancient Times: 
      Laws of lever, Archimedean screw, Archemedes principle, Spherical shape of earth,
      Determination of radius of earth, Planets vs stars, distances to planets
2. Heliocentric Theory:
Copernicus model, Keplers laws, Newtons law of universal gravitation
3. Newtonian Mechanics:
Newtons laws of motion, Application to rigid bodies, Application to continuous systems,
Mechanical view of universe
4. Thermodynamics:
Energy conservation and first law of thermodynamics, One way flow of heat and second law of thermodynamics
5. Field Point of View: 
The development of the concept of field, Faradays law of induction, Maxwells equations and the unification of electricity, magnetism and optics. Man-made electromagnetic waves, wireless communication and development of electronics, Path to special relativity
6. Quantum Ideas:
           Early radiation theories and ultraviolet catastrophe, Planck radiation formula, 
           photoelectric effect and photon hyposthesis, Bohr atom and deBroglie hypothesis

PH 117.desc:
1.	Lab Manual
2.	Advanced Practical Physics, Worsnop and Flint

PH 113.desc: Simple Harmonic motion, damped SHM, critical damping, Sustaining oscillations in a damped oscillator. Driven oscillation, resonance, damped-driven oscillator and its resonance, Q-factor, Vanderpol oscillator, non-linear feedback for sustained oscillations. SHM in 2-dim, dependence on initial conditions, Lissajous figures, condition for closed orbits, SHM in 3-dim. Oscillations of two particle systems, symmetric and asymmetric modes, general solution to the problem. Driven oscillations of two particle system. Oscillations of `n` particle systems, normal modes, Formulation of the general problem, eigenvalues and eigenvectors of normal modes, general solution for arbitrary initial conditions. Driven oscillations. Example of a linear triatomic molecule. Longitudinal and transverse oscillations, modding out the zero frequencies. Oscillations of a chain of `n` atoms. Continuum limit, vibrational modes of a string of constant density. Equation of Motion for waves, Standing waves and travelling waves in 1 dimensions. Properties of waves in two and three dimensions Harmonics, Linear superposition of harmonics, odd harmonics, construction of pulse shapes. Fourier components of a periodic pulse, Fourier analysis and Fourier coefficients. Fourier analysis of arbitrary functions, Fourier Coefficients.

PH 221.desc:
1. Fundamentals of semiconductor of device physics
2. Electronic signal transmission in circuits in time and frequency domain
3. Operational characterization of diodes and transistors performance.
4. Use of a transistor as a switch
5. Study linear amplification mode of transistor with some applications including:
a.	voltage amplification
b.	power amplification
6. Opamps  Design characteristics 
7. Typical opamp application circuits including:
a.	Inverting, Non-inverting voltage amplification
b.	Transimpedance amplifiers to measure signals from typical transducers like photo-diodes, thermocouples etc
c.	Instrumentation amplifier and/or Lock-in amplifier
Feedback control circuit using the PID (Proportional-Integrative-Differential) algorithm

PH 227.desc:
Syllabus:
Data Science: Introduction to data driven systems and examples.
Bayesian vs. Frequentist statistics. Random variables, probability
distribution with continuous and discrete outcomes, common distribution
functions. Moments and moment generating function. Central limit
theorem, parameter Inference: maximum likelihood method and
confidence interval.
Hypothesis testing: Test statistic, chi-squared test, p-value test.
Introduction to linear models, simple and multiple linear regression,
logistic regression.
Machine Learning: What is learning? Learning objective, data needed.
Introduction to supervised learning, training validation and testing
(including loss function) - (i) Decision trees and support vector machines
(SVM), classification and regression trees (CART)  growing trees,
variable importance measure (ii) Random Forests - separating
hyperplane, maximum margin classifier, support vector classifier. (iii)
Introduction to deep learning: Definition and types of neurons, neural
network architecture, cost function, feed forward neural network, back
propagation algorithm. Artificial Neural networks (ANN), Deep Neural
Networks, Convolution Neural Networks (CNN). Applications of neural
networks (deep learning) in optimization, regression and model building.
Multivariate analysis and dimension reduction. Singular Value
Decomposition (SVD), Principal Component Analysis (PCA) etc.

PH 223.desc:
Part A (60% of the course):  Complex analysis 

12)	Complex numbers z.  Complex plane.  Triangle inequalities. 
13)	Continuity, Differentiability,  Continuity and existence of partial derivatives, Cauchy-Riemann conditions, pure complex function of z.  
14)	Analyticity.  Single value.  Cauchys theorem.  Complex Taylor Series. Convergence and domains of analyticity. Order of zeros. 
15)	Cauchy integral formula,  and derivation of Laurent Series. Calculation of Residues. 
16)	Meromorphic functions and order of poles, Branch singularities, Essential singularities. 
17)	Residue theorem. Cauchys argument principle. 
18)	Various types of contour integrals  semicircles, rectangular, conical.  Case of poles on real line. 
19)	Branch Points and branch cuts.  Integrals involving branch singular integrands. 
20)	Shapes of complex functions and Saddles.  Darbouxs inequality.  Proofs of impossibility of local maxima and minima. Impossibly of entire & bounded function.
21)	Asymptotic analysis:  Laplaces method, stationary phase, and method of steepest descent. 
22)	Conformal Mapping and properties.   Linear and Inversion map and their geometric effects on lines and circles. Application to 2d electrostatics. Logarithmic map.  Homographic transformations and cross-ratio preservation.  

Part B (40% of the course):  Function spaces, Integral transforms and some Differential equations

7.	Infinite dimensional vector spaces or function spaces. Inner product, and weight function. The problem of completeness. Riemann to Lebesgue integrals,  and Lebesgue space. Reisz-Fisher theorem. Bessel inequality,  and Persevals equality. Hilbert space. 
8.	Weierstrasss theorem and polynomial basis. Orthonormalization of polynomials using Schmidt method. Generalized Rogriguess formula and 3 classes of classical polynomials. 
9.	Good, fairly good,  and generalized distributions (namely, Dirac delta). Continuous index basis and use of Dirac delta.  Identity operator and  completeness relation of polynomials.
10.	Fourier series as a basis expansion. Fourier cosine and sine series. Fourier transforms. Plancherel-Parseval relations. Meaning of Fourier transforms of generalized functions like Dirac delta where Parseval relation fails. 
11.	Examples of Fourier transform calculations  reminding complex analysis. Transforms of derivatives and derivatives of transforms. Solving linear ODEs,  and PDEs (like diffusion equation) using Fourier transform. Convolution theorem. 
12.	Laplace transforms.  Examples.  Derivatives of transforms and transforms of derivatives.  Shifting properties. Solution of linear ODEs and PDEs.  Convolution theorem. Inverse Laplace transforms, Bromwich integrals and contour integration.

PH 217.desc: Review of Newton`s laws of motion, frames of reference, rotating frames, centrifugal and Coriolis forces. Free and constrained motion, D`Alemberts principle and Lagrange`s equation of first kind. Lagrangian formulation, Hamiltons equation of motion. Variational principles. Canonical transformation and Poisson Bracket. Hamilton Jacobi theory and action angle variables.Periodic motion, small oscillations, normal coordinates, Central force, Kepler`s Laws and Rutherford scattering.

PH 251.desc: Review of Newtons laws of motion. Hamiltons principle, variationalmethod  and Lagranges equations with and without constraints.  Centralforce, Keplers laws.  Hamiltons equations, canonical transformation,Poisson brackets. Periodic motion, small oscillations, normal coordinates.Rigid body dynamics, moment of inertia tensor, Euler equations, motion of asymmetric top.  Frames of reference, rotating frames, centrifugal andCoriolis forces.

PH 253.desc: Review of thermodynamics: notion of equilibrium, equation of state, first and second laws of thermodynamics, thermodynamic potentials and Maxwell`s relations. Phase space, ergodicity, Liouvilles theorem, microcanonical, canonical and grand canonical ensembles, Boltzmann statistics and its applications to ideal gas. Bose-Einstein and Fermi-Dirac statistics, and their applications.

PH 307.desc:
1. Basics: Errors, binary representation of numbers.

2. Solutions of algebraic equations: bisection method, false-position method, modified false-position method, Newton-Rapson method both for one and many variables

3. Interpolation and extrapolation: forward, backward, and divided differences and the corresponding Newtons interpolation formulas. Lagrange formula for interpolation.

4. Numerical integration: finite differences based approaches such as trapezoidal rule, Simpson rule, and Romberg integration. Gaussian quadrature.
5. Solutions of ordinary differential equations: Euler method and improved Euler method, Runge-Kutta class of methods up to fourth order. Predictor-Corrector methods

6. Solutions of Partial Differential Equations: Mainly finite difference approaches, spectral methods employing the basis-set expansion techniques. 

7. Matrix Algebra: Solution of systems of linear equations using Gaussian Seidel and Gaussian elimination approaches. LU factorization. Iterative approaches to systems of linear equation. Diagonalization of matrices using Jacobi rotation, power method, Rayleigh quotient approach, and inverse iteration mehod. 
Inverse of a matrix using different methods such as Fadeev-Leverrier method

8. Random Number generation and Monte Carlo Methods for calculations of integrals. Metropolis algorithm. Kinetic Monte Carlo Method.

9. Fast Fourier transform (FFT): Fourier transform of discretely sampled data, FFT for real functions, Sine and Cosine functions. Convolution and deconvolution using FFT, correlation and auto-correlation using FFT

PH 309.desc:
Addition of angular momentum, Clebsch-Gordan coefficients, Tensor operators, Wigner Eckart theorem.

Approximation methods in Quantum Mechanics: (i) Variational principle,  (ii) WKB method, (iii) time-independent and (iv) time dependent perturbation theory. 

Applications in atomic physics: Hydrogen atom Fine structure -Relativistic effect, spin orbit coupling, hyperfine interaction. Stark, Zeeman and Paschen-Back effects. Ground and excited states of helium, direct and exchange terms. 
Time-dependent electromagnetic interaction, transition probabilities and selection rules. Scattering theory-Born approximation. Partial wave analysis.

A brief exposure on Klein-Gordan and Dirac equation.

PH 353.desc: Maxwells equations and propagation of light, Fourier analysis, Lorentz model of optical response, optical response of various natural and artificial materials, metamaterials, photonic crystals, polarization of light, scattering phenomena, lasers, nonlinear light-matter interaction, ultrafast phenomena, strong light-matter interaction, plasmonics, and few applications of light-matter interactions like photovoltaics and optical switching

PH 403.desc:
1. Experimental Motivation, Future of Quantum Mechanics. Axioms of QM.
2. Mathematical Preliminaries: Linear Algebra (esp. trace, partial trace, tensor products. . . ), Hilbert Space,
Orthogonal Polynomials, Rotations &Unitaries.
3. Quantum States & Density Matrices.
4. Schr ̈odingers equation, Schr ̈odingers equation for Unitaries, Schr ̈odinger, Heisenberg & Interaction
Pictures.
5. Simple Problems in One Dimension, Preview of Selection Rules.
6. Harmonic Oscillators, Uncertainty Principle, Ladder Operators.
7. Spin-1/2: Qubit states, Bloch Sphere Representation, Transitions, Rabi Oscillations. . .
8. Coupled Quantum Systems. . .
9. Central Force Problems, Rigid Rotor. . .
10. Hydrogen Atom, Angular Momentum Operators, Addition of Angular Momentum.

PH 401.desc:
1.	A short revision of elementary Newtonian mechanics  - Forces, Torques, Rotational motion, Sliding friction, Rolling friction as a constraint, Oscillations of  single degrees of freedom, Energy conservation, Energy non-conservation and inelasticity. 

2.	Lagrangian formalism
(i) Generalised Kinetic energy, Potential energy, Symmetries leading to Lagrangian of a free particle in non-relativistic and relativistic mechanics. 
(ii) Hamiltons principle, Variational method, and Euler-Lagrange equations. 
(iii) Symmetries and conservation laws  generalised momenta, energy function, and Gauge  freedom of Lagrangian. 
(iv) Lagrangian systems with constraint  Lagrange multipliers, and generalised forces of  constraint and virtual work. 
(v) Particles to fields  derivation of 1-dimensional wave equation, and electromagnetic Lagrangian to Lorentz force law and Maxwells equations. 

3.	Coupled Oscillators and small oscillations
Lagrangian formulation of linearly coupled systems  normal modes and normal frequencies. Examples. 

4.	Hamiltonian formulation
(i)Derivation of the Hamiltons equations of motion. Hamiltonian of certain systems, specially of a  particle in an electromagnetic field. 
(ii) Phase space flows in second-order autonomous systems. Special case of Classical Hamiltonian systems  comparison to incompressible fluids. Examples of Hamiltonian phase  space flows  elliptic and hyperbolic fixed points. 
(iii) Gauge freedom of the Lagrangian and corresponding changes in the Hamiltonian. 

5.	Central forces 
(i)Differential and integral equations of orbit. 
(ii)Conditions for bounded orbits or/and closed orbits. Precession of the axis of ellipse. 

6.	Canonical Transformations
(i)Motivation, condition for canonical transformation, and types of generating functions.
(ii) Symplectic criterion for Canonical Transformations. 
(iii) Infinitesimal Canonical transformations, Generators. 
(iv) Poisson bracket invariance, and Jacobis identity. 
(v) Phase space volume conservation, and Liouvilles Theorem. Hamiltons Jacobi equation. Discuss the quantum to classical limit. 

7.  Rigid bodies

(i)Frames of reference  accelerating and rotating frames and pseudo-forces.
(ii)Rigid body motion with one point fixed. The orthogonal rotation matrix  its components, determinant, and eigenvalues. Eulers theorem. Finite rotation formula. Infinitesimal rotation matrix, and concept of angular velocity. 
(iii) Velocity of different points of a rigid body. Motion split into that of the Centre of mass, and about the Centre of mass  angular momentum, and kinetic energy formulas. The inertia tensor. Its components related to the symmetries of the rigid body. Parallel axis theorem. 
(iv) The body frame, Euler angles and Euler equations. 
(v) The symmetric top  force and torque free motion, and motion under constant gravity.

PS 643.desc:
PS 643-Introduction to AI, Data, and Policy is one of the two new courses, one introductory PS643 and one advanced PS 644 which replaces the old course PS 626-AI, Data and Policy which has been running for four years as an institute elective. 

PS 644 offered in Spring will have PS 643 as a prerequisite. 

While PS626 was only available to grad students, PS643 is also open to final year undergrads.

This course is an interdisciplinary introductory course for AI and policy. It will bring the student up to scratch with the landscape of extant policymaking on AI as well as make certain they learn the basic concepts, digital and philosophical, of AI, machine learning, and data, learn to code a small AI artefact, learn about basic AI and data policy and governance structures as they exist in India and worldwide, and the ongoing debates on the same. By the end of the course, the student would have learnt a basic amount of what AI technologies are, how data interacts with them, how they influence and are influenced by the world, and how to design policy around them. Students with a background in technology but wishing to learn the social impact of AI, or conversely with a background in the social sciences wishing to understand the technology of AI, will be benefited from this course. There are no prerequisites to this course but there are expectations the student will learn.

Topics to be covered:

     Basic ML, NLP, and CV
     AI, its History, and Concepts
     Extant AI and Data Governance
     Fairness, Accountability, Transparency, Ethics, Human Rights
     Indian AI Policy
     Data Regulation, EU and India
     AI and Surveillance
