PS 660.desc: Course Outline Documentaries for Policy Change Week 1-2  What is a Documentary? Colonial filmsThe course opens by situating documentary filmmaking within broader historical and political contexts. Students will explore the origins of the documentary as a medium to capture "fragments ofactuality starting with early films in India such as Panorama of Calcutta (1890s) and Washing a Baby (1905). These early works serve as entry points into the study of Colonial Film-cinema made under and for empire, often used to support imperial policy and shape public perception and policy. Key screenings, including A Road in India, will help students understand the colonial gaze and its narrative control. The week introduces the Films Division of India as a postcolonial institutional counterpart, continuing the legacy of using film to frame politicalideology. Reading Camille Deprez`s The Films Division of India, 1948-1964 provides essential context on the British documentary tradition`s influence on India`s state media. Students will explore the archives on Films Division`s YouTube channel and colonialfilm.org.uk to critically engage with how visual media has historically interacted with policy objectives. Week 3-4: State Sponsored Cinema and the Nation-Building Project These weeks deepen the discussion on Films Division of India as a cultural instrument of the newly independent India. Through screenings and analyses of films like I Am 20 (based in IIT Bombayalumni) and A Few More Questions, students will examine how documentaries articulated visions of modernity, national development. and civic identity.The course will explore how the state used documentary form to influence public policy discourse on youth, education, industry, andnationhood, focusing on films that often sought to construct a unified national identity amidst socio-political diversity. Focus Filmmakers such as SNS Sastry, Pramod Pati, and Sukhdev will be studied as auteurs who negotiated state directives withpersonal and political expression. Week 5-6. The Rise of the Indian Independent DocumentaryThe shift to independent documentary filmmaking in the 1980s marks a critical turn in both form and function. Students will explore how independent filmmakers providing alternative accounts of sociopolitical realities. Special attention will be given to Cinema V303251rit303251 and observationalstyles that emphasized authenticity and minimal intervention. Screenings such as Chronicle of a Summer (1961) and Grey Gardens (1975) will inform this discussion. Week 7 Documenting Culture-Between Tradition and Modernity This module explores the use of documentary to understand and narrativize cultural change, subcultures, and disappearing traditions.Students will examine films that use avant-garde, experimental, and reflexive techniques to go beyond ethnographic realism.Screenings: Mati Manas, Siddheshwari (Mani Kaul), Lovely Villa (Avijit Mukul Kishore and Rohan Shivakumar), and Bidesia in Bambai (Surabhi Sharma) showcase how filmmakers representurbanization, migration, and cultural memory within changing policy environments. These films offer new ways to visualize the intersections of culturalpolicy, heritage, and the lived experience of modernization.Week 8: Agrarian and Rural FuturesScreening: Moti Bagh (Nirmal Chander)Through a deeply personal portrayal of an aging farmer in Uttarakhand, Moti Bagh enables students to engage with agrarian policy, land use, rural development schemes, and environmentalsustainability. The film offersapoignant critique of the structural failures affecting Indian agriculture and raises questions about policy priorities and demographic transitions in rural India Week 9 Urban Policy This session will examine global housing inequality, displacement.and urban gentrification through a policy lens. Push exposes the collusion between multinational finance and local urban governance. illustrating how documentary films can critique neoliberal urbanplanning and advocate for housing as a right.Week 9-10 Documentary as an ethnographic Method for Public Policy This section introduces visual anthropology and the ethnographic filmtradition. Students will evaluate how ethnographic methods are adapted in documentary practices to investigate everyday life, rituals. and social structures. Core Screening: When Four Friends Meet allows a comparative discussion on genre, authorship, and reflexivity, while raisingquestions about representation, positionality, and subjectivity in public narratives. Discussions will focus on how ethnographic films can inform orcomplicate public understandings of social development policies, indigenous rights, and participatory governance. Week 11-12: Ethics, Representation in Documentary filmsThe course concludes with an in-depth look at the ethical dilemmas in documentary filmmaking. Students will interrogate the filmmaker`sresponsibility in representing trauma, marginality, and resistance. Key Screening: The Look of Silence (2014) by Joshua Oppenheimer serves as a focal point for discussions on truth-telling, reconciliation,and justice. Topics will include consent, agency, power asymmetries, and the documentary`s role in shaping historical memory and influencingpost-conflict policy. Conclusion: Documentary as Policy Intervention By the end of the course, students will have a comprehensive understanding of how documentary films function not just as art orreportage, but as powerful interventions in policy-making and public debate. Students will leave with the tools to analyze documentary cinema as a space where state, society, and storytelling converge.Evaluation: Attendance and Classroom Participation -20% End-Term Exam: Documentary Review Essay -25% Students will submit a critical review of two documentary films screened during the course, evaluating their policy relevance, aesthetic strategies, and impact. Emphasis should be on linking the films to broader contexts and course themes such as representation, governance, or development. Project Proposal & Treatment Note -30% Students will develop a concept note for a documentary idea aimed at a specific policy intervention (e.g., urban housing, education reform,environmental sustainability). Weekly Reflection Notes -25% (In-class/takehome) Students will submit short critical reflections (300-400 words) on five screenings across the semester (of their choice). These reflections should focus on linking form and content, and exploring the film`srelationship to state policy or public discourse.

PS 661.desc:
Data science enables governments to optimize the allocation of public resources, evaluate the effectiveness of policies, and enhance service delivery across sectors such as health, education, and infrastructure. In addition to traditional econometric approaches, data science leverages advanced statistical and machine learning techniques to reach quicker and more precise results. Further, it facilitates cross-departmental insights, enabling departments to collaborate and share knowledge, leading to more holistic and impactful governance.Understanding the scope, methods, and applications of Data Science in Governance equips students of public policy with the means to analyse and manage large datasets, build models, track policy outcomes, generate actionable insights, and communicate these insights effectively. This course intends to bridge the gap between technical skills and policy analysis, fostering an understanding of the ethical and practical considerations involved in data use in governance, and ultimately supporting more responsive and inclusive governance practices.

1. Overview of data science and its role in policy analysis.a. How data science informs evidence-based policy makingb. Types of data: survey, administrative, open data.2. Basic concepts in statistics and probability. a. Descriptive and inferential statistics.b. Sampling and bias.c. Hypothesis testing.d. Discussion on observed biases in Indian socio-economic surveys and their implications3. Data collection, sorting, and cleaning.a. Digital Enterprise systems.b. Record Linkagec. Data wrangling: outliers, anomalies, missing data, deduplication.d. Ethics in data collection, storage, and use.e. Bias, fairness, transparency and interpretability, privacy.Case studies from Indian public databases- UIDAI, Aarogya Setu4. Building models.a. Regression models: DiD, RCT, IV, PSM, RDD etc.i. Discussed with reference to their application in relevant econometric papers.b. Spatial data.i. Nightlights and GDP datac. Supervised classification models.i. Policy targeting, etc.d. Unsupervised models: Clustering, biplots.i. Education/ Awareness studies5. Exploratory Data Analysis.a. Trends and patterns. Causation and correlation. 6. Data Communication.         a. Principles of good data visualisation.        b. Condensing and communicating insights from data analysis to a range of stakeholders.7. Historical records and NLP.8. Handling and analyzing big data.        a. Cloud computing.        b. Distributed data.

PS 663.desc:
This course provides a critical overview of the practice of urban planning, the historical underpinnings of its disciplinary knowledge, the urban challenges in the Indian context and planning`s responses. It provides a necessary framework to understand the institutional armature for all urban development and policy initiatives. The perspective of the course is critical and technical. It illuminates the explicit and tacit knowledge underlying the practice of urban planning in India. Through a key mapping exercise, (outlined in the practical component section below) students will understand how abstract policy instruments and tools capture reality, which then informs policy and planning. The course is also designed to enable students to understand urban challenges, innovative and sustainable planning responses to them through case studies.Broad contents History of urban planning (international and in India)
3 Tier Governance structure and institutional arrangements Planning Disciplinary Knowledge (theory and technical knowledge) Urban Challenges in the Indian city Responses: Case studies of planning responses in India and the Global South
(Curitiba, Goa, Pune, Bangalore, Mumbai etc)
Future of Urban Cities Practical Component
Aim To critically evaluate a tool employed by planners in drawing up Development Plans- the Existing Land Use Maр Task Each student will prepare an Existing Land use map for a 1 km long stretch of street and properties abutting both sides of the street. While choosing the street area the choice has to be such that it is an important street or one which has multiple uses, in other words a busy street with both formal and informal uses,ideally with some mix of uses.
1. A land use map for the street/area chosen has to be made based on site visits after
2. recording land uses based on observation of uses that the land is being put to. This task will include
a. Observations recorded on map showing street uses at different times of the day
b. Maps showing uses that the properties abutting the street are put to
c. If there are multiple uses in the building on different floors, try and record them
(if you are not allowed inside the property look at signboards and record the
uses) Use photographs to capture the uses as you walk along the street - make sketch maps recording uses
d. Develop appropriate ELU categories based on the uses you have captured in the
site visits and uses found.
e. Distil all uses that are similar and creating categories to capture these uses and
creating categories for the ones not similar
f. Creating a map of the uses found in the study area-the Existing land use map
Expected submission: Site visit mapping done (observations sheets) (PDFS); A final
land use map (drawn over an open street map or google map) based on site visits
recording the land uses A clear list of final categories and sub categories (if any); A
short two page analysis reflecting on the experience of doing the exercise and the difficultiesencountered while thinking of categories. (PDFs)

SC 631.desc:
Basics of static games: Zero-sum and non-zero sum games,
                   concept of Nash equilibrium and Stackelberg equilibrium.
                   Multi-act games: extensive form of games and information
                   sets. Aumanns common knowledge, rationality, bounded
                   rationality. Dynamic games: Incomplete information,
                   Bayesian Nash equilibrium. General formulation of dy-
                   namic games: sub-game perfectness, open-loop, closed-
vii Course Content
                   loop and feedback Nash equilibria, informational proper-
                   ties of Nash equilibria, informational nonuniqueness. Infor-
                   mation structures: static and dynamic information struc-
                   tures. Dynamic stochastic team problems: introduction,
                   person-by-person optimality, Witsenhausen problem, sig-
                   nalling, connections to economics and information theory.

SC 639.desc:
The broad objective of this course is to introduce three fundamental
branches of mathematics
- vector spaces, analysis and multivariable calculus -
that are extremely beneficial for advanced engineering applications and
studies.

* Vector spaces (6 weeks): Linear independence, basis, dimension, subspaces,
linear functionals, dual spaces, linear transformations, inner products,
projections,the least squares problem, normed vector spaces, eigen values and eigen
vectors.

* Sequences and series (4 weeks): Sequences, series and convergence, Cauchy
sequences,tests for convergence, power series, differentiation and integration of a series.

* Multivariable calculus (4 weeks): Taylor's formula, differentiation of functions of several variables,
Gateaux and Frechet derivatives, differentiability of vector-valued functions, the chain rule, Taylor's formula for functions of several variables, the inverse function and implicit function theorems.

SC 651.desc: Course: SC *** - Estimation on Lie Groups (3 0 0 6)Instructor: Ravi Banavar (Room No.110, Ext. 7888),Systems and Control Engineering (situated behind the Mathematics department.)Prerequisites: An understanding of vector spaces (or MA 108 or SC 639)Rough Outline: Kalman filtering: Studying R. E. Kalman302222s first two papers on linear filter-ing and prediction problems. Manifolds, Lie groups and bundles: Introduction to the machinery ofmanifolds and Lie groups, group actions, the adjoint and co-adjoint actions,the infinitesimal generator, fiber bundles Structure of estimators on Lie groups - Setting the framework for at-titude estimation in satellites and spacecraft, Visual Simultaneous and Local-ization (VSLAM) in mobile robotics, working out the structure and design of observers in both cases.302225 Extended Kalman Filters (EKF) on Lie groups - Understanding anddeveloping the notion of linearization on a Lie group and the equivalent EKF.

SC 655.desc: 1. Random variables, density and distribution functions for univariate and multivariate random variables.     2. Gaussian random vectors, minimum mean square error estimation, Kalman filter; applications in system theory.     3. Elementary concentration inequalities, Gaussian concentration, Johnson-Lindenstrauss; applications to robust optimization and dimension reduction.     4. Discrete Markov chains, Markov times, ergodicity of Markov chains, sampling, sampling and Markov chain Monte Carlo, simulated annealing; applications to robust and global optimization.     5. Martingales and stochastic approximation; applications to stochastic gradient descent.

SC 625.desc: *Basic of linear algebra :vector spaces and matrices*Solution to linear ODEs:uniqueness, state-transition matrix, peano-baker series variation of constants formula* Stability: Lyapunov stability, stability of BIBO Stability*Controllability: LTV systems, controllability Gramian Controllability matrix, Hautus test, Lyapunov test, pole placement.* Stabilizability: Controllable decomposition, stabilizability tests, state feedback control*Observability:Observability Gramian, duality for LTI systems, observability tests* Detectability:Observable decomposition, detectabilty tests, state estimators. output feedback control, separation principle*Kalman decomposition, minimal realization*Optimal control: Dynamic programming HJB equation, LQR problem, Riccati differential equation, algebraic Riccati equation, maximum principle.

SC 663.desc:
1.Matrix Calculus and Algebra
2.Network Methods
3.Sparse and Structured Optimization 
4.Engineering and ML Applications

SC 658.desc:
Analytical mechanics
- Vectors and frames
- Newtons laws for systems and particles and rigid bodies
- Constraints, generalized coordinates
- Virtual work principle, generalized forces, 
- Extension of dynamics (D-Alembert principles), Lagranges Equations, Rouths equations
- Variational Principles,
- Conserved quantities and Cyclic coordinates, Generalized momenta, 
- Hamilton/Jacobi integral, Hamiltons equations
Aero/hydro dynamic forces
Identification
- Discrete-time dynamical systems 
- AR, ARX model estimation via least squares
- AR-MAX

SC 899.desc: This course will consist of a series of seminars/lectures delivered by both faculty members and teaching assistants and also talks streamed from online repositories. The topics covered in the course could include surveying literature, listening and note taking, technical writing, speaking and presentation skills, elevator pitch, ethics in academic communication, group discussion and interviews and report writing using LateX.

SC 664.desc: This course will focus on the design of controllers for mitigating the effect of vibration on engineering systems. The topics covered are as follows: Control systems preliminaries (state-space and transfer function approaches), applications of active vibration control, vibration control via linear regulator equations (state-feedback design, error-feedback design, internal model principle, robustness), repetitive control technique, low-gain controller designs, adaptive/tuning vibration controllers, H-infinity techniques, vibration control via nonlinear regulator equations (center manifold theorem, immersion, solving regulator equations).

SI 419.desc: Counting Basic Combinatorial objects: Sets, Multisets, Partitions of sets, Partitions of numbers, Permutations, Trees, Partially ordered sets.Generating functions, Recurrence relations, Principle of Inclusion-Exclusion.Graph Theory Graphs and Directed graphs, Paths, Walks, Connectivity, Matchings in bipartite graphs, Network flows, Dilworths theorem.

SI 424.desc: Distributions of functions of random variables, Sampling distributions, Order statistics, Sufficiency and completeness, exponential family of distributions, Methods of estimation (Method of Moments, MLE and Bayesian), Unbiased estimators, Evaluating estimators, UMVUEs, Testing, Likelihood Ratio tests, UMP tests, unbiased tests, Interval estimation, Consistent and efficient estimators.

SI 423.desc:
Vector spaces (with emphasis over R and C):
Subspaces, linear dependence and independence, basis and dimension.

Linear transformations: Rank-nullity theorem, matrix representation of a linear transformation, invertibility and isomorphism, effect of change of basis on the matrix representation of a linear transformation, dual spaces.
Review of elementary properties of determinants, Cramer`s rule.

Diagonalization: Eigenvalues and eigenvectors, algebraic and geometric multiplicities of an eigenvalue, diagonalizability, invariant subspaces and Cayley-Hamilton theorem.

Inner product spaces: Gram-Schmidt orthogonalization, adjoint of a linear operator, normal and self-adjoint operator, orthogonal projections and the spectral theorem, singular value decomposition and pseudoinverse, bilinear and quadratic forms.

Canonical forms: Jordan canonical form (with emphasis on computation).

SI 427.desc:
Random phenomena, sample spaces, events, sigma algebra, probability space, properties of probability, conditional probability, independence, Bayes formula, Polya`s urn model.

Discrete random variable, probability mass function, independent random variables, sum of random variables, random vector, expectation of discrete random variable, properties of expectation and variance.

Continuous random variable, distribution function, density of a continuous random variable, expectation, change of variable formula, random vector, joint distribution of random variables, joint density, distribution of sums and products of random variables, conditional density, conditional expectation, order statistics, moment generating function, characteristic function, brief introduction to moment problem.

Inequalities: Markov, Chebyshev, Schwarz and Chernoff bound.

Convergence in probability, almost sure convergence, convergence in distribution, relation between these three modes of convergences, weak law of large numbers (WLLN), strong law of large numbers (SLLN), central limit theorem (CLT).

SI 429.desc:
Review of sequences and series of real
numbers. Limit superior and limit inferior, Cauchy
sequences and completeness of R.
Tests for convergence of series of real numbers.

Basic notions of Metric Spaces with emphasis on R n . Heine Borel Theorem.

Continuity and Uniform continuity.
Derivatives. Mean Value Theorem and applications. Functions of bounded variation.

Riemann-Stieltjes integral. Improper integrals and Gamma function.
Sequences and series of functions. Uniform
convergence, interchanging limits with integrals
and derivatives. Arzela-Ascoli theorem (statement only).

Functions of several variables: Partial
derivative, directional derivative, total derivative; Mean value theorem, Taylor`s theorem.

SI 503.desc:
Two-way contingency tables: Table structure for two dimensions. Ways of comparing proportions. Measures of associations. Sampling distributions. Goodness-of-fit tests, testing of independence. Exact and large sample inference.

Models of binary response variables. Logistic regression. Logistic models for categorical data. Probit and extreme value models. Log-linear models for two and three dimensions. Fitting of logit and log-linear models. Log-linear and logit models for ordinary variables.
Regression: Simple, multiple, non-linear regression, likelihood ratio test, confidence intervals and hypotheses tests, tests for distributional assumptions Collinearity, outliers, analysis of residuals. Model building, Principal component and ridge regression. Lab component: Relevant real life problems to be done using statistical Software Packages such as SAS etc.

SI 431.desc:
Overview of R software, Data Frames, R Scripts, creating, importing/exporting and merging of datasets, creating matrices and basic matrix operations in R, 2d/3d plotting, programming in R (for, if else, do and while loops), functions, creating report using R markdown.

Exploring data using R, Scatter plot, histogram, bar chart, pie chart, box plot, basic statistics computation (mean, median, variance etc.) Generating random samples from standard distributions (such as Bernoulli, Poisson, Normal, Exponential etc.)  and comparing theoretical pdfs/pmfs using histograms/frequency distributions, quantiles of sampling distributions (t, chi and F distribution) Maximization/minimization of functions in R (some algorithm), MLE estimation.

Polynomial fitting of scatter plot, introducing regression line, least squares estimates, residual plots, testing normality of residuals (qqplot), goodness of fit measures and tests, testing of regression parameters, simulation of regression model, empirical distribution of least square estimator and its comparison with theoretical distribution.

Simulation of multivariate normal random vectors, estimation of mean and covariance matrix, eigen values and eigen vector of variance covariance matrix, spectral decomposition covariance matrix. Generating dependent random variables with some models like (random walk, AR(1), MA(1) etc).

SI 507.desc: Principles of floating point computations and rounding errors.Systems of Linear Equations: factorization methods, pivoting and scaling, residual error correction method.Iterative methods: Jacobi, Gauss-Seidel methods with convergence analysis, conjugate gradient methods.Eigenvalue problems: only implementation issues.Nonlinear systems: Newton and Newton like methods and unconstrained optimization.Interpolation: review of Lagrange interpolation techniques, piecewise linear and cubic splines, error estimates.Approximation : uniform approximation by polynomials, data fitting and least squares approximation.Numerical Integration: integration by interpolation, adaptive quadratures and Gauss methodsInitial Value Problems for Ordinary Differential Equations: Runge-Kutta methods, multi-step methods, predictor and corrector scheme, stability and convergence analysis.Two Point Boundary Value Problems : finite difference methods with convergence results.Lab. Component: Implementation of algorithms and exposure to public domain packages like LINPACK and ODEPACK.

SI 543.desc:
1. Review of convergence concepts in probability.
2. Delta-method and variance stabilizing transformations.
3. Asymptotic properties of sample moments and quantiles and related inference procedures. Asymptotic distribution of order statistics and extreme observations.
4. Asymptotic efficiency of estimators. Asymptotic properties of the likelihood-based estimators and related inference procedures.
5. Implementing asymetric techniques for data analysis.

SOM601.desc: This course includes components for self reflection and assessment, interpersonal interactions and groupactivities. Indicative key contents are: Dynamics of values, belief and attitude and behaviour, Emotionalintelligence, Listening skills, Assertiveness, Conflict management, Negotiation skills, Team and group dynamicsCommunications: An Introduction; Meaning, nature and characteristics of communications (art, science andprocess); The communication process, the role of feedback; Objectives and functions of communication; Levelsand types of communication; Essentials of good communication- Seven C302222s of communication; Barriers incommunication; Difference between effective and ineffective communication; Noise in communications; How todevelop good listening skills?Verbal, Non-verbal communications and Written Communications (from the perspective of businesscommunications); Oral and written presentations; Public Communications: Organizing, outlining and deliveringthe speech; The informative speech and the persuasive speech; Conducting effective meetings; Writtencommunications in business (Emails, letters, reports, abstracts and so on)

SI 505.desc: K-variate normal distribution. Estimation of the mean vector and dispersion matrix. Random sampling from multivariate normal distribution. Multivariate distribution theory. Discriminant and canonical analysis. Factor analysis. Principal components.Distribution theory associated with the analysis.

SOM603.desc:
Introduction - Scope of Financial, Cost and Managerial Accounting. Accounting Principles  GAAP, AS, IFRS.
Accounting Mechanics. Balance Sheet, and Income Statement. Cash flow Statement.Ethical Issues in
Accounting. Cost terms and Concepts, Marginal Costing, CVP, BEP Analysis, Budgeting, Standard Costing.

SOM602.desc:
Module 1: The Fundamentals of Microeconomics: Goals and Constraints ,The Nature and Importance
of Profits, Understanding Incentives, Understanding Markets, Time and Value of Money, Marginal
Analysis, Basic Calculus: The Calculus of Optimization.
Module 2: Demand and Supply: Demand ,Supply ,Market Equilibrium, Price Ceilings and Price Floors,
Comparative Statics: Changes in Demand and Supply, Price Elasticity of Demand, Price Elasticity, Total
Revenue, and Marginal Revenue, Factors Affecting Price Elasticity, Cross Price Elasticity, Income
Elasticity of Demand, Other Elasticities, Module 3: Theory of Consumer Behaviour: Choice and Utility
Theory, Law of Diminishing marginal utility, Consumer Equilibrium, Indifference curve Analysis,
Consumer Surplus, Substitution and Income Effects.
Module 4: Costs of Production and the Organization of the Firm: The Production Function, Profit-
Maximizing Input Usage, Isoquants and Isocosts, Cost Minimization and Optimal Input Substitution,
The Cost Function .Long-run Costs and Economies of Scale.
Module 5: Market Structure: Pricing and Output Decisions: The Nature of Industry, Perfect
Competition, Monopoly , Monopolistic Competition and Oligopoly
Module 6: The Economics of Information and the Role of Government in the Marketplace Asymmetric
Information Auctions, The Role of Government in the Marketplace.

SOM101.desc: Introduction to the course and importance of management for an engineer.Introduction to Business, Industrial Business, Classification of Industries and Job Opportunities (referring the industries visiting our campus).Who is a manager?, brief discussion on classification of managers (i.e. top, middle and lower), managers and administrators, definitions of management (Peter F Drucker`s, Harold Koontz`s, and Universally accepted definitions), Functions of Managers/Management and time spent on various managerial functions by managers at various levels. F W Taylor`s, Henri Fayol`s definition of management; Importance of management, features (characteristics) of management, Efficiency and Effectiveness, Productivity.Managerial Skills, the roles played by managers ,Systems Approach to Management, Management is science or art.Planning: Difference between general planning and managerial planning. Major types of Planning (Strategic, Tactical and Operational) and major types of Plans (Mission, Vision, Policies, Procedures, Strategies, Rules, Projects), and Steps in Planning. Planning: Process of Management By Objectives (MBO), Guidelines in setting the objectives, Key result areas, Qualitative and Quantitative Objectives. Study: Setting objectives in key result areas (as per Peter F Drucker).Strategic Planning: Planning Tools (Critical Question Analysis, SWOT and TOWS Analysis). Strategies at Corporate, Tactical and Operational levels; Successful implementation of strategies. Balance Score CardStrategic Planning: Business Portfolio Analysis and Porter`s Model. What is Strategic Planning? And the Process (steps in Strategic Planning). Different types of stakeholders and their interests, Fiscal and Social ResponsibilitiesOrganizing: What is Organizing? and the Process, Principles of Organizing , Span of Management.; Organisation Design and CultureOrganizing: Line and Staff concepts (Line, Staff and Functional Staff authority), A few structures (Line and Staff; and Project and Matrix) in detail, Delegation (Definition, Principles and Steps). Detailed study on Line, Functional and Committee Structures, Difference between formal and informal organization, Types of staff managers.Leading: What is? Differences between - Leading and Managing, Leader and Manager; Differences between - motives, motivators and motivation. Theories of Motivaton - Maslow`s Need Hierarchy, Herzberg`s Two-Factor Theory and McGregor X and Y.Leading: Motivational Theories continued, Motivational TechniquesLeading: Leadership Styles, Likert`s Four Systems of Management, Ingredients of Leadership, Theories, Leadership Grid.Leading: Communication - Difference between General and Managerial Communication, Types of Communication, Controlling: what is? and the Process (steps), Management Control Techniques (Budgetory, Non-budgetory and Network) and types of control. Who should make budgets? Classification of budgets into Financial, Operational and Non-monetary.Functions of an entrepreneur, Types of entrepreneurs; Traits and characteristics of Entrepreneurs & Intrapreneurs, Business Plans; Innovation Management for Business Success, Organisation LifecycleProfessional Ethics 302227 Senses of Engineering Ethics, Variety of moral issues, Types of inquiry, Moral dilemmas, Moral Autonomy,  Moral Leadership, Code of Conduct, Corporate Social Responsibility (CSR).Global Issues 302227 International Management and Multinational Corporations, international competitiveness

SOM624.desc:
The course aims to develop techniques/skills that are critical in the design and operations of warehouses.
Topics covered
 The role of warehousing in contemporary supply chain; types of warehouses; warehouse processes.

 Overview of warehouse design and operation problems.
 Warehouse activity profiling.

 Warehouse operation: receiving and shipping; SKU assignment to departments; storage location
assignment; zoning; order picking- batching, routing and sorting.
 Measuring and benchmarking warehouse performance; warehouse best practices.
Warehouse management system

economic lot sizing models, sequence dependent set up cost optimization, KANBAN systems, integer

A review of probability and statistics. Simple and multiple regression. Multicollinearity, heteroscedasticity,

Key Elements and Decision Categories in an Operations Strategy Capacity, Supply Network, Process

SOM727.desc:
1. Introduction to Applied Quality Engineering & Management 
    2. Quality of Design: Kano Model, FMEA (Failure Mode & Effect Analysis), Product Reliability, Robust Design 
    3. Service Quality 
    4. Acceptance Sampling Plan
    5. Statistical Quality Control (Variable and Attribute Charts) 
    6. Process Capability Analysis 
    7. Design of Experiments: Single and Multiple Factor Experiments
    8. Multiple Response Optimization 
    9. Measurement System Analysis (MSA) 
    10. Taguchi Method 
    11. Lean Six Sigma Methodology
    12. Total Quality Management (TQM) and ISO 9000 series

          Pedagogy: The lecture will be supplemented with different cases and hands-on classroom exercises. Minitab and R statistical software will be used for demonstration and solving cases.

SOM726.desc:
Introduction; Economic Thought; Historical context; Industrial Revolution and wheels of growth and development; Growth Theories (classical, neo-classical as well as modern ones); Growth-Development Debate; Poverty and Inequality including Inequality of Opportunity; Growth and Inequality Debate; Capability Approach and Multidimensional Poverty; Inequality in Education and Health; Development Planning in International Context vs. India; Human Development in India; Costs of Economic Development in India and Political Economy of Development in India; Cognitive Capture, Elite Capture and Regulatory Capture.   

Tutorials will be there for all topics. Now for every topic (for example, poverty inequality etc.,) hands on experience on estimation will be provided immediately using national/state level data and a software. So, survey reports and code book will also be discussed.

SOM741.desc: Introduction to Financial Statements and Analysis Framework, Analysis of Balance Sheets, Income Statements and Cash Flow Statements, Analysis of Income Tax, Financial Reporting Quality, Financial Analysis Techniques, Intercorporate Investments, Multinational Operations, Analysis of Employee Compensation, Introduction to Analysis of Financial Institutions.

SOM739.desc:
1. Introduction to AI - Algorithms, Networks, Data, and Machine Learning in Marketing 
       2. Introduction to AI and Marketing - Evolution of AI and marketing automation 
       3. Fundamentals of Machine Learning in Marketing - Basic principles of machine learning algorithms and their application in marketing for customer segmentation, targeting, and forecasting 
       4. AI-Driven Market Research and Customer Insights - Application of NLP in sentiment analysis, social listening, and customer feedback 
       5. AI for Customer Journey Mapping 
       6. Applications of GenAI in Marketing (Content Creation, Customer Support, Advertising) 
       7. AI in Brand and Product Development - AI driven innovation in product ideation, design, and development, from generating ideas to optimizing brand identity 
       8. Personalization and AI in Advertising through behavioral targeting, dynamic content delivery, and automated ad optimization 
       9. Chatbots and AI in Customer Experience (CX) Optimization 
       10. Ethical Considerations in Use of AI in Marketing such as data privacy, algorithmic biases, and transparency issues 
       11. Emerging AI Technologies and the Future of Marketing - Big Data Analytics and AI Forecasting, Social Listening and Sentiment Analysis 
       12. The Future of AI in Marketing - Use of virtual reality (VR), augmented reality (AR), and the metaverse in customer engagement, advertising, and personalization.

SOM709.desc: The International Business Imperative: Economies in Transition, Economic Integration; National Trade and Investment Policies; International Business Entry; Multinational     Corporations.    Strategies      for Globalization. The Global Strategic Frameworks, Management of International Business. Global Strategic Planning. Prospects and Problems for Globalization ofIndian Firms.

SOM749.desc:
NOTE: The concepts introduced in this course (in comparison to the existing Consumer Behaviour and Consumer Neuroscience course) have been highlighted in Yellow. 

        The contents of the course can be broadly sub-divided into two: 

     (i) Consumer psychology and consumer behaviour: An overview to understanding consumer behaviour and underlying thought processes will be covered in the first part.  
The concepts covered here would include theories related to consumer`s motivation & goals; personality, perceptions, learning & memory, consumer attitude formation and change, persuasion, social and cultural setting. Sensory marketing and its applications in consumer psychology and behaviour will also be covered in detail with case studies. Role of self and self-identity in consumption will also be covered. Also, the course would cover topics and case studies on role of emotions and mood in decision-making and theories related to affect; consumer behaviour in the digital age; children as consumers; consumers and environment. 

     (ii) Consumer neurosciences: Brain operating principles, decision-making in the brain; understanding the application of the principles and techniques of neurosciences to marketing; measurements of neural evidences when using EEG and fMRI; facial coding and protocols; uncover consumer implicit responses (training on how to conduct laddering interviews) and decision-making at sub-conscious and unconscious levels; understanding the connection between brain, emotions and brands; understanding the principles and methodology of eye tracking to study the sub-conscious decision-making of consumers. Hands-on training on Tobii Pro Fusion (250 Hz) eye tracker will be provided to students. 

     Marketers` Ethics and Social Responsibility will also be covered.

TD 603.desc:
Principles of Watershed Management:  Basic concepts, Hydrology and water availability, Surface water, Groundwater, Conjunctive use, Human influences in the water-resources system, Water demand, Integrated water resources system  River basins.  Watershed management practices in arid and semi-arid regions, Watershed management through wells, Management of water supply  Case studies, short term and long term strategic planning
Conservation of Water:  Perspectives on recycle and reuse, Waste water reclamation
Social Aspects of Watershed Management:  Community participation, Private sector participation, Institutional issues, Socio-economy, Integrated development, Water legislation and implementation, Case studies

Water Harvesting:  Rainwater management- conservation, storage and effective utilization of rainwater, Structures for rainwater harvesting, roof catchment system, check dams, aquifer storage.  Applications of Geographical Information System (GIS) and Remote Sensing (RS) in watershed management, Role of Decision Support System (DSS) in watershed management

TD 609.desc: In this course, students will be required to undertake approximately 10-week stay in a rural region.  They will be located either with a local NGO or a local Govt office.  The students will be required o make social observations and keep records in a diary.  The objective is to familiarize with a rural setting as planners for a region or a public utility project.  The students will make observations on resource-technology-need linkages (forward and backward) and learn to incorporate them in an in-house system dynamics model to assess impacts of different technological interventions on future sustainability of regions.  They will be required to defend the best-practise scenario generated by them using the model.

TD 638.desc:
1. What is Development?

Theories and Models: Neo-liberalism, Washington Consensus, New Institutional Economics
6. Development and Social Equity: Basic Needs Approach, Human Development, Capabilities Approach

TD 613.desc:

History of Environmental Issues:  History of environmental problems, Local, regional & global issues, Pollution and its control, Environmental regulation and innovation
Environmental Management:  Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA), Environmental audits, Industrial ecology, Pollution prevention, Green design, Eco-friendly lifestyle

TD 651.desc: In this course, each year, one technology (e.g. Brick making, Telephoney, Fishing, Dairying) will be discussed in historical and contemporary settings highlighting economic, cultural, political, scientific and technological determinants of change.  The course will draw upon outside consultants, historians, journalists and academics.
